verses.dev: ॐ सुकेशा च भारद्वाजः शैब्यश्च सत्यकामः सौर्यायणी च गार्ग्यः कौसल्यश्चाश्वलायन उद्दालकश्च भार्गवः कबन्धी च कात्यायन एते ब्रह्मपरा ब्रह्मनिष्ठाः परं ब्रह्मान्वेष्यन्तो भगवन्तं पिप्पलादमुपसन्नाः ॥
verses.iast: om sukeśā ca bhāradvājaḥ śaibyaśca satyakāmaḥ sauryāyaṇī ca gārgyaḥ kausalyaścāśvalāyana uddālakaśca bhārgavaḥ kabandhī ca kātyāyana ete brahmaparā brahmaniṣṭhāḥ paraṁ brahmānveṣyanto bhagavantaṁ pippalādamupasannāḥ ||
verses.trans: Om. Sukeśā Bhāradvāja, Śaibya Satyakāma, Sauryāyaṇī Gārgya, Kausalya Āśvalāyana, Uddālaka Bhārgava, and Kabandhi Kātyāyana — these six, wholly devoted to Brahman and grounded in Brahman, seeking the highest Brahman, approached the venerable Pippalāda carrying sacrificial fuel in hand.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara notes that the six disciples are qualified seekers — not mere ritualists but those already established in brahma-niṣṭhā, steadfast in Brahman. Their gathering before Pippalāda signifies the traditional guru-śiṣya relationship indispensable for Vedāntic inquiry. The phrase 'paraṁ brahmānveṣyantaḥ' indicates they seek Brahman beyond all attributes — nirguṇa Brahman — which cannot be reached by ritual alone but only through direct knowledge (jñāna). The fuel (samit) symbolises readiness to kindle the fire of inquiry.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja's tradition sees the six disciples as representing the ideal devotee — someone already grounded in scriptural knowledge yet humble enough to approach a realised teacher. Their seeking of 'paraṁ brahma' is the seeking of Viṣṇu-Nārāyaṇa, the Supreme Brahman who is the inner ruler (antaryāmin) of all souls. Approaching with samit signifies self-surrender (śaraṇāgati), the prerequisite for grace. Pippalāda as guru is himself an instrument of divine compassion.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva holds that Brahman here refers unequivocally to Viṣṇu, who is absolutely independent (svatantra) and the sole object worthy of enquiry. The disciples are categorically distinct from Brahman — their 'brahma-niṣṭhā' means devotion to Viṣṇu, not identity with Him. The Praśna Upaniṣad opens, from the Dvaita perspective, with a scene illustrating the eternal, unbridgeable ontological difference between jīva and Īśvara, even as the jīva earnestly seeks the Lord.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees the gathering of the six as representing different facets of the seeking soul — intellect, devotion, enquiry — all converging on the single question of ultimate reality. Pippalāda embodies the integral realiser who can guide the evolving consciousness upward. The Upaniṣad begins with a social image of the spiritual community (āśrama), which Aurobindo regards as the ancient form of the collective yoga — many individualities united in a common aspiration toward the transcendent.

verses.dev: तान्ह स ऋषिरुवाच भूय एव तपसा ब्रह्मचर्येण श्रद्धया संवत्सरं संवत्स्यथ अथ यथाकामं प्रक्ष्यथेति ॥
verses.iast: tānha sa ṛṣiruvāca bhūya eva tapasā brahmacaryeṇa śraddhayā saṁvatsaraṁ saṁvatsyatha atha yathākāmaṁ prakṣyatheti ||
verses.trans: The sage said to them: "Dwell with me for yet one year more, with austerity (tapas), celibacy (brahmacharya), and faith (śraddhā). Then ask whatever questions you wish."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara explains that Pippalāda does not reject the disciples but purifies them further. One year of brahmacharya, tapas, and śraddhā removes the mental impurities (mala) and agitation (vikṣepa) that obstruct Self-knowledge. The teacher's first gift is not information but a method of inner preparation. True Vedāntic knowledge cannot penetrate a mind unpurified by discipline — this is why even already-qualified seekers need further seasoning in a teacher's presence.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: From Rāmānuja's viewpoint, the year of preparation is an act of divine grace mediated through the guru. Tapas, brahmacharya, and śraddhā are not mere ethical requirements but forms of devotional practice that draw the grace of the Lord inward. The teacher tests whether the students' desire for liberation is genuine or merely intellectual. Only when śraddhā (trust in the teacher and in the scriptures) is steady can the instruction bear fruit, since the knowledge of Brahman must be received with the right disposition.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva emphasises that even highly learned disciples must undergo purification before the highest truths are disclosed. The year's discipline cultivates the vairāgya (dispassion) and śraddhā (faith in Viṣṇu's supremacy) without which even correct scriptural statements cannot take root. In Dvaita, knowledge alone is insufficient — it must be accompanied by unshakeable faith in Viṣṇu's absolute independence and the soul's dependence on Him. This probationary year instils exactly that.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads this verse as affirming that spiritual knowledge demands a prepared instrument. The year is not an arbitrary test but an organic process of transformation — tapas as concentrated will-force, brahmacharya as conservation of vital energy, śraddhā as the soul's alignment with truth. In the Integral Yoga framework, no true spiritual opening occurs without this triple purification. Pippalāda's method is thus a prototype of the integral discipline that transforms the whole being, not merely the intellect.

verses.dev: अथ कबन्धी कात्यायन उपेत्य पप्रच्छ । भगवन् कुतो ह वा इमाः प्रजाः प्रजायन्त इति ॥
verses.iast: atha kabandhī kātyāyana upetya papraccha | bhagavan kuto ha vā imāḥ prajāḥ prajāyanta iti ||
verses.trans: Then Kabandhi Kātyāyana approached and asked: "Venerable sir, from what do all these creatures come to be born?"

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara treats this as the foundational cosmological question: what is the first cause of phenomenal existence? The answer will reveal that creation (sṛṣṭi) is ultimately a projection of Brahman/Ātman through the appearance of duality — prāṇa and rayi. For Advaita, however, creation is ultimately mithyā (neither real nor unreal in the absolute sense) — Kabandhi's question is valid from the vyāvahārika (conventional) standpoint but dissolves at the pāramārthika (ultimate) level where Brahman alone is.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja would hold that this question is asked in full sincerity: the world of creatures is genuinely real, and its origin is genuinely worth knowing. The answer reveals the creative will of Brahman (Īśvara), who out of sport and compassion wills the emergence of the cosmos. Souls (cit) and matter (acit) are the Lord's body; their origination is an act of the Lord's freedom, not an illusion. Kabandhi's question thus opens the great theme of how the one Lord becomes the many without ceasing to be one.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: From Madhva's perspective, Kabandhi correctly seeks the ultimate cause of creation, which Madhva identifies as Viṣṇu's independent will. Creation is not an emanation from Brahman's substance (which would compromise divine purity) but an act of free lordship. Jīvas and jagat are eternal categories distinct from Viṣṇu, though wholly dependent on Him. The question 'kuto ha vā imāḥ prajāḥ' is thus the most important question — its answer establishes the ontological hierarchy: Viṣṇu supreme, souls and matter subordinate.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees in this primordial question the soul's own deepest drive — to understand its source. In the Integral framework, creation is the descent of the Infinite Spirit into matter, a self-concealment with the purpose of a greater self-revelation. The prajāḥ (creatures) are not accidents but expressions of the divine will to manifest and delight in infinite forms. The question 'from where?' is not merely intellectual curiosity but the soul recognising its own foreignness to the world it inhabits.

verses.dev: तस्मै स होवाच प्रजाकामो वै प्रजापतिस्तपोऽतप्यत स तपस्तप्त्वा स मिथुनमुत्पादयते रयिं च प्राणं चेत्येतौ मे बहुधा प्रजाः करिष्यत इति ॥
verses.iast: tasmai sa hovāca prajākāmo vai prajāpatistapо'tapyata sa tapastaptvā sa mithunamutpādayate rayiṁ ca prāṇaṁ cetyetau me bahudhā prajāḥ kariṣyata iti ||
verses.trans: He said to him: "Prajāpati, the lord of creatures, desiring offspring, performed austerity (tapas). Having performed tapas, he produced a pair — rayi (material form/matter) and prāṇa (life-force) — thinking: 'These two shall produce creatures for me in many ways.'"

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara interprets Prajāpati here as Hiraṇyagarbha — the cosmic Self at the level of saguṇa Brahman — who, under the power of māyā, appears to desire and create. The primal pair of prāṇa and rayi corresponds to puruṣa and prakṛti in Sāṃkhya terminology, though ultimately both are appearances within Brahman. Tapas here signifies the concentrated intentional power of Brahman through which the apparent duality of subject and object, consciousness and matter, is projected. The Advaita reading reminds us that the desire mentioned is phenomenal, not a real limitation in Brahman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees Prajāpati as the Lord Himself, who out of His own fullness and playful will (līlā) desires the multiplication of the world of souls. Rayi and prāṇa are the Lord's material and vital aspects — both genuinely real and both forming the cosmic body of God. The act of tapas is the Lord's own meditative self-expression, for He creates not from lack but from overflowing abundance. The pair (mithuna) of rayi and prāṇa reflects the inseparability of matter and spirit in Viśiṣṭādvaita — neither exists independently of the Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads Prajāpati as Brahma, the secondary creator (and himself a jīva of highest rank), acting under the directive power of Viṣṇu. Rayi and prāṇa are Viṣṇu's own creative energies manifesting as matter and life. Their duality is real and eternal — matter and life-force are never identical with each other or with God. Tapas performed by Prajāpati is possible only because Viṣṇu empowers him, underscoring that no creative act happens independently of the Supreme.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads prāṇa and rayi as the primordial polarisation of the Life-force and Matter, the two terms between which all evolution plays out. The universe begins with the one divine consciousness dividing itself into dynamic prāṇa (the active, luminous principle) and static rayi (the receptive, material principle). Tapas represents the concentrated divine will — śakti in its focused form — that initiates cosmic manifestation. Aurobindo's evolutionary vision sees this duality as the starting condition for a progressive involution and then re-emergence of spirit from matter.

verses.dev: आदित्यो ह वै प्राणो रयिरेव चन्द्रमाः रयिर्वा एतत्सर्वं यन्मूर्तं चामूर्तं च तस्मान्मूर्तिरेव रयिः ॥
verses.iast: ādityo ha vai prāṇo rayireva candramāḥ rayirvā etatsarvaṁ yanmūrtaṁ cāmūrtaṁ ca tasmānmūrtireva rayiḥ ||
verses.trans: "The sun is indeed prāṇa; the moon is indeed rayi. Rayi is all this — both the formed and the formless. Therefore form itself is rayi."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara reads this cosmic correspondence — sun as prāṇa, moon as rayi — as establishing that the entire visible cosmos is the manifestation of these two fundamental principles. All that has form (mūrta) and all that is formless (amūrta) are ultimately rayi — i.e., the material substratum. From the Advaita standpoint, even the distinction between formed and formless is within māyā; when the superimposition is removed, Brahman alone is, neither prāṇa nor rayi but their ground. This correspondence is a teaching device to show the macro-micro unity.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees in the identification of sun with prāṇa and moon with rayi a demonstration of God's pervasion of all natural phenomena. The sun and moon as cosmic manifestations of prāṇa and rayi belong to the Lord's body; everything formed and formless is sustained by His inner ruling presence. This verse supports Viśiṣṭādvaita's samanvaya (harmonisation): the cosmos is neither an illusion nor independent of God, but is God's own real body, animated by the divine prāṇa.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets the sun and moon as presiding deities directly energised by Viṣṇu. Prāṇa (the sun's energy) and rayi (the moon's nourishing matter) are Viṣṇu's śakti (power) acting in distinct modes. The distinction between formed (mūrta) and formless (amūrta) rayi is real — matter exists in many genuine categories, none of which is illusory. This verse, for Madhva, underscores the real plurality of the cosmos while tracing all plurality to a single divine source.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo emphasises the solar-lunar polarity as a cosmological expression of the dynamic (prāṇa) and static (rayi) aspects of the universal Life-force. The sun's radiant energy and the moon's reflective material quality together encompass all existence. Both the formed and the formless are expressions of the cosmic Material-principle (rayi) which Spirit has entered. In Aurobindo's reading, the recognition of this duality is preparatory — the seeker must understand the structure of creation before they can transcend it.

verses.dev: अथादित्य उदयन्यत्प्राचीं दिशमनुप्रविशति तेन प्राच्यान्प्राणान्रश्मिषु संनिधत्ते यद्दक्षिणां यत्प्रतीचीं यदुदीचीं यदधो यदूर्ध्वं यदन्तरा दिशो यत्सर्वं प्रकाशयति तेन सर्वान्प्राणान्रश्मिषु संनिधत्ते ॥
verses.iast: athāditya udayanyatprācīṁ diśamanupraviśati tena prācyānprāṇānraśmiṣu saṁnidhatte yaddakṣiṇāṁ yatpratīcīṁ yadudīcīṁ yadadho yadūrdhvaṁ yadantarā diśo yatsarvaṁ prakāśayati tena sarvānprāṇānraśmiṣu saṁnidhatte ||
verses.trans: "When the sun rises and enters the eastern quarter, it thereby gathers the prāṇas of living beings in the east within its rays. When it illumines the south, the west, the north, below, above, and the intermediate directions — by illuminating everything it gathers all the prāṇas within its rays."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara uses this verse to teach that all prāṇas (vital energies) of all beings are ultimately rays of the one cosmic prāṇa, symbolised by the sun. Just as rays cannot exist apart from the sun, all individual vital forces are fundamentally one with the cosmic Self. This is a preparatory non-dual teaching: the multiplicity of individual prāṇas is appearance; their unity in the solar prāṇa points toward the unity of all consciousness in Brahman. The gathering of prāṇas in the rays is an image of the dissolution of individual identity back into the source.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees the sun as an instrument of the Lord through which He sustains all living beings simultaneously. The prāṇas of all creatures in all directions being held within the sun's rays expresses God's universal immanence and omnipresence. No creature exists outside the reach of the divine sustaining power. The verse establishes the Lord's role as universal upholder (viśvadhāraṇa), a cornerstone of Viśiṣṭādvaita theology — the cosmos is sustained moment to moment by God's active will, not mechanically.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva sees Sūrya (the sun-deity) as a high-ranking deva who acts as Viṣṇu's agent in sustaining the prāṇas of all living beings. The gathering of individual prāṇas within the solar rays reflects the hierarchical cosmos in Dvaita: Viṣṇu at the apex, Sūrya as intermediary, and individual souls as subordinate recipients of divine power. No soul generates its own prāṇa independently — life-force is always a bestowed, contingent gift, underscoring the absolute dependence of jīvas on the Lord.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads this verse as a great cosmic image of the Supermind distributing its rays of consciousness and life to all quarters of existence. The sun as prāṇa gathers all individual vitalities within itself, showing that the cosmic Life-force is one, not many. In the Integral Yoga perspective, this verse describes the universal Prāṇa-Śakti that underlies all biological and psychic life. Evolution, for Aurobindo, is the progressive clarification of this universal prāṇa from its obscuration in matter toward its fullest expression in the supramental light.

verses.dev: स एष वैश्वानरो विश्वरूपः प्राणोऽग्निरुदयते तदेतदृचाभ्युक्तम् ॥
verses.iast: sa eṣa vaiśvānaro viśvarūpaḥ prāṇo'gnirудayate tadetadṛcābhyuktam ||
verses.trans: "He — the universal fire (Vaiśvānara), of universal form, rises as prāṇa and fire." This is expressed in the following verse:

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara identifies Vaiśvānara with the cosmic prāṇa that manifests as fire in the digestive system and as the sun externally. The 'universal form' (viśvarūpa) points to the Brahman-nature of prāṇa — it is not a limited vital force but the all-pervading consciousness appearing as life-energy. This verse connects the macrocosm (the sun) to the microcosm (the internal fire of digestion), a characteristic Upaniṣadic teaching that the outer and inner cosmos are identical in principle, both being expressions of the one Ātman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees in Vaiśvānara (literally 'the fire belonging to all men' or 'the universal fire') an expression of the Lord's immanence within every being's digestive and metabolic processes. The Lord dwells as the inner fire that transforms food into energy, sustaining all life from within. This verse supports the Viśiṣṭādvaita doctrine that the Lord is the antaryāmin (inner ruler) of all natural processes — not a distant creator but the intimate sustainer of every organism.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets Vaiśvānara as Agni, the fire-deity, who is a direct manifestation of Viṣṇu's power. The term 'viśvarūpa' does not imply non-difference between prāṇa and Brahman but shows that Viṣṇu's energies take all forms in the cosmos. Fire, both cosmic and digestive, is always a dependent expression of Viṣṇu's śakti, not a self-sufficient principle. This prevents any monistic confusion between the instrument (prāṇa-agni) and the ultimate agent (Viṣṇu).
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees Vaiśvānara as the universal Life-fire that underlies all energetic processes in the cosmos. Rising as prāṇa, it is the vital-fire that animates both the macro-cosmic sun and the micro-cosmic metabolic flame. In Aurobindo's integral cosmology, fire is the first symbol of the ascending movement of consciousness — matter igniting into life, life into mind, mind into spirit. The verse thus encapsulates the entire evolutionary trajectory in the single image of Vaiśvānara rising.

verses.dev: विश्वरूपं हरिणं जातवेदसं परायणं ज्योतिरेकं तपन्तम् । सहस्ररश्मिः शतधा वर्तमानः प्राणः प्रजानामुदयत्येष सूर्यः ॥
verses.iast: viśvarūpaṁ hariṇaṁ jātavedasaṁ parāyaṇaṁ jyotirekaṁ tapantam | sahasraraśmiḥ śatadhā vartamānaḥ prāṇaḥ prajānāmudayatyeṣa sūryaḥ ||
verses.trans: "This sun rises as the prāṇa of all creatures — universal in form, golden-hued, all-knowing, the supreme goal, the one blazing light — the one with a thousand rays, moving in a hundred ways."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara comments that this verse encapsulates the cosmic prāṇa's nature: universal (viśvarūpa), omniscient (jātavedas, the one who knows all births), the supreme resort (parāyaṇa), and one undivided light (jyotirekam). The 'thousand rays' indicates infinite manifestations of a single source — a classic Advaita teaching on the one appearing as many. The sun rising as prāṇa of all creatures shows that individual prāṇas are not truly separate; they are rays of one cosmic light, which is itself a pointer to the non-dual Ātman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads this beautiful verse as a hymn to the Lord's immanence in the solar prāṇa. The sun as 'jyotirekam' — the one undivided light — reflects God's unity, while its 'sahasraraśmiḥ' — thousand rays — reflects the plurality of creatures sustained by Him. Jātavedas (knower of all births) expresses God's omniscience; parāyaṇam (supreme resort) expresses His role as the ultimate refuge for all souls. The verse is thus both cosmology and devotional affirmation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva sees Sūrya here as the great deva who, empowered by Viṣṇu, acts as the life-sustainer of all beings. The epithet 'parāyaṇa' (supreme resort) could be taken as Viṣṇu Himself shining through the solar form. The thousand rays are Viṣṇu's infinite powers (śaktis) working through the solar instrument. For Madhva, the verse establishes a clear hierarchical mediation: Viṣṇu → Sūrya-deva → prāṇa of creatures, with no collapsing of one level into another.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo finds in this verse a vision of the Supermind as the solar consciousness — thousand-rayed, omniscient (jātavedas), blazing with the light of Truth. The sun as 'parāyaṇa' (the highest goal) and 'jyotirekam' (the one light) expresses the Supramental Truth-Consciousness that illumines all levels of existence simultaneously. 'Moving in a hundred ways' captures the infinite adaptability of divine consciousness as it guides the evolution of life in all its forms.

verses.dev: संवत्सरो वै प्रजापतिस्तस्यायने दक्षिणं चोत्तरं च तद्ये ह वा एतद्यज्ञेन दानेन तपसेति दक्षिणमयनमनुयन्ति ते चन्द्रमसमेव ते पुनरावर्तन्ते तस्मादेत ऋषयः पितृयाणमप्रशंसन्ति ॥
verses.iast: saṁvatsaro vai prajāpatistasyāyane dakṣiṇaṁ cottaraṁ ca tadye ha vā etadyajñena dānena tapaseti dakṣiṇamayanamanu yanti te candramasameva te punarāvartante tasmādeta ṛṣayaḥ pitṛyāṇamapraśaṁsanti ||
verses.trans: "The year is Prajāpati; it has two paths — the southern and the northern. Those who worship through sacrifice, charity, and austerity by the southern path win the world of the moon — and they return again. Therefore the sages who desire offspring do not praise the southern path (pitṛyāna)."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara interprets the two paths as the pitṛyāna (the way of the ancestors, leading through the moon) and the devayāna (the way of the gods, leading to non-return). The southern path, associated with ritual and merit, leads to the temporary enjoyment of celestial worlds followed by rebirth — it is the path of karma, not jñāna. Śaṅkara here echoes the Chāndogya Upaniṣad's teaching on the five fires (pañcāgni-vidyā). The sages do not praise this path because it perpetuates saṃsāra — temporary liberation is not liberation at all.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja acknowledges the pitṛyāna as a real path leading to real but temporary rewards. Those who worship through yajña, dāna, and tapas without directing them toward the Supreme Lord gain the lunar world and return. This path is not wrong but incomplete — it lacks devotion (bhakti) directed to Īśvara. Rāmānuja's Vedānta values ritual action when performed as worship of the Lord (Īśvara-arpaṇa) — only then does it lead to lasting liberation rather than cyclical return.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads the two paths as reflecting the actual cosmic structure of post-mortem journeys. The southern path leads the soul to Candra-loka (the lunar world) where it enjoys the fruits of its meritorious actions before returning. This path is associated with worship of deities other than Viṣṇu — legitimate but inadequate for liberation. The sages do not praise it not because it is evil but because it leaves the soul still within the cycle of birth and death. Only the path of Viṣṇu-bhakti leads to Vaikuṇṭha and no return.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees the two paths as stages of spiritual ascent. The southern path (pitṛyāna) represents the soul's journey through the vital-mental worlds built by accumulated karma — real experiences but temporary and cyclic. The sages' refusal to praise it is not a condemnation but a pointing beyond. In the Integral perspective, the soul must eventually transcend the cycles of the vital cosmos and attain the supramental transformation, which alone is permanent. The lunar world is a symbol of the mental-vital level of consciousness — luminous but reflected, not the original solar truth.

verses.dev: अथोत्तरेण तपसा ब्रह्मचर्येण श्रद्धया विद्ययात्मानमन्विष्यादित्यमभिजयन्ते एतद्वै प्राणानामायतनमेतदमृतमभयमेतत्परायणमेतस्मान्न पुनरावर्तन्त इत्येष निरोधस्तत एष श्लोकः ॥
verses.iast: athottareṇa tapasā brahmacaryeṇa śraddhayā vidyayātmānamanviṣyādityamabhijayante etadvai prāṇānāmāyatanam etadamṛtamabhayametaparāyaṇametasmānna punarāvartanta ityeṣa nirodhastata eṣa ślokaḥ ||
verses.trans: "But by the northern path, through austerity, brahmacharya, faith, and knowledge, those who seek the Self win the sun. That (sun) is the abode of all prāṇas, the immortal, the fearless, the supreme resort. From it they do not return. This is the final resting-place." Then this verse:

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara considers the northern path (devayāna) as the path of jñāna leading to Brahmaloka and, ultimately, to videhamukti (liberation at death without further rebirth). The sun here is not the physical sun but the effulgent Self — the sūrya-maṇḍala in which the Puruṣa of golden hue is seen by the dying sage. 'From it they do not return' echoes the Chāndogya and Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣads. For Śaṅkara, this verse points directly to non-dual liberation: the distinction between the seeker and the sun dissolves in the realisation of Brahman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees the northern path as the path of upāsanā (meditation) combined with bhakti — the sincere seeking of the Self (ātmānveṣaṇa) through disciplined practice. The sun (Āditya) won by this path represents the highest reality accessible through devotional meditation: the Brahman who is Nārāyaṇa. 'From it they do not return' indicates the attainment of sālokya or sāyujya — the final state of dwelling in or merging with the divine, which Rāmānuja interprets as the blessed communion of the liberated soul with the Lord, not ontological identity.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva holds that the northern path leads to mokṣa — the eternal, blissful service of Viṣṇu in Vaikuṇṭha. The sun won by this path is the transcendent abode of Viṣṇu (Vaikuṇṭha), not the physical sun. 'From it they do not return' means the liberated jīva never re-enters the cycle of saṃsāra; it remains eternally distinct from Viṣṇu yet filled with divine bliss. The required means — tapas, brahmacharya, śraddhā, and vidyā — are all forms of Viṣṇu-focused sādhanā in the Dvaita reading.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads the northern path as the soul's ascent through the higher mental and supramental planes to the solar Truth-consciousness. Tapas, brahmacharya, śraddhā, and vidyā are the four pillars of the complete yoga that enables this ascent. 'The abode of all prāṇas' reveals that the solar consciousness is not the negation of life but its source and fullness. Non-return from the sun means the complete transformation of consciousness — not an escape from existence but its fullest realisation, the point at which evolution has achieved its purpose.

verses.dev: पञ्चपादं पितरं द्वादशाकृतिं दिव उत्तरार्धे परे मन्यन्ते अथेमे अन्य उ परे विचक्षणं सप्तचक्रे षडर आहुरर्पितमिति ॥
verses.iast: pañcapādaṁ pitaraṁ dvādaśākṛtiṁ diva uttarārdhe pare manyante atheme anya u pare vicakṣaṇaṁ saptacakre ṣaḍara āhurarpit amiti ||
verses.trans: "Some regard him as the Father with five feet and twelve forms, abiding in the upper half of the sky — others see him as the far-sighted one, set in the wheel of seven wheels with six spokes."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara interprets the 'five feet' as the five seasons, the 'twelve forms' as the twelve months, and the 'upper half of the sky' as the devayāna. The 'seven wheels' are the seven worlds or seven metres of Vedic verse; the 'six spokes' are the six seasons. This cosmic imagery of Prajāpati as time encodes the Vedic vision of the cosmos as a sacrificial wheel. For Advaita, these numerical symbols ultimately point beyond time to the timeless Brahman who is the ground of all cyclical manifestation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads this verse as a cryptic description of the Lord as the cosmic Person who governs time. The five feet (five seasons), twelve forms (months), seven wheels (sapta-chandas or the seven worlds), and six spokes (six ṛtus) all belong to the Lord's cosmic body. The verse celebrates the Lord's identity with time itself — 'kālo'smi loka-kṣaya-kṛt pravṛddho' as the Gītā says. This cosmic vision is the preliminary awe that leads the devotee toward personal devotion.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva takes this verse as describing Sūrya (or the cosmic Prajāpati acting under Viṣṇu's direction), whose structure reflects the architecture of divine creation. The numbers are not merely astronomical but encode the sacred geometry of Viṣṇu's creative will. Multiple traditions (marked by 'some say... others say') indicate that different schools perceive different aspects of the same supreme reality. Madhva insists that these are all valid partial visions of Viṣṇu's inexhaustible cosmic form.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo finds in this verse a vision of the cosmic time-spirit — the Father who is also the year, the seasons, and the celestial revolutions. The five feet and twelve forms represent the temporal framework within which the divine unfolds its manifestation. The seven wheels with six spokes evoke the chakra imagery that appears throughout the Vedic and Tantric traditions, pointing to the spiralling structure of the universe's creative energy. Time, for Aurobindo, is not the enemy of spirit but the field of its progressive self-revelation.

verses.dev: मासो वै प्रजापतिस्तस्य कृष्णपक्ष एव रयिः शुक्लः प्राणस्तस्मादेते ऋषयः शुक्ल इष्टिं कुर्वन्त्यितरस्मिन्निति ॥
verses.iast: māso vai prajāpatistasya kṛṣṇapakṣa eva rayiḥ śuklaḥ prāṇastasmādete ṛṣayaḥ śukla iṣṭiṁ kurvantyitarasmin iti ||
verses.trans: "The month is Prajāpati. Its dark (kṛṣṇa) fortnight is rayi; its bright (śukla) fortnight is prāṇa. Therefore these sages perform their ritual works in the bright fortnight; others do so in the dark fortnight."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara continues the cosmological correspondence: the month encodes the same prāṇa-rayi duality as the sun and moon. The bright fortnight is associated with the ascending, vital, solar principle (prāṇa); the dark fortnight with the descending, material, lunar principle (rayi). Sages who understand this choose the bright fortnight for ritual work because the prāṇa-force is ascendant. This teaching is ultimately aimed at revealing that all cosmic cycles are expressions of the one prāṇa-rayi duality, which itself resolves into Brahman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja notes that the monthly cycle of bright and dark fortnights mirrors the greater cosmic duality of prāṇa and rayi. The prescription for sages to perform iṣṭi (fire-ritual) in the bright fortnight reflects the importance of aligning ritual action with cosmic rhythm — an expression of harmony between the individual worshipper and the Lord's cosmic order (ṛta). Ritual action performed in alignment with cosmic cycles is more efficacious in drawing the divine grace that leads toward liberation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva sees the month as another expression of Viṣṇu's cosmic governance through time. The alternation of bright and dark fortnights is divinely ordained for the proper ordering of ritual life. Sages perform in the śukla pakṣa (bright fortnight) to take advantage of the ascendant divine energy; this is not mere superstition but a recognition of the real differences in cosmic energy that Viṣṇu has built into the structure of time. Ritual performed in harmony with this structure is more directly connected to Viṣṇu's grace.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees the lunar cycle as a rhythm of the universal vital force — the bright fortnight as the ascending wave of prāṇa-śakti, the dark fortnight as its descending withdrawal into material latency. The sages' preference for the bright fortnight for ritual work reflects an intuitive sensitivity to these cosmic vital rhythms. In the Integral framework, the practitioner of yoga learns to work with such rhythms rather than ignoring them, understanding that the universal Life-force has its tides that can assist or hinder inner work.

verses.dev: अहोरात्रो वै प्रजापतिस्तस्याहरेव प्राणो रात्रिरेव रयिः प्राणं वा एते प्रस्कन्दन्ति ये दिवा रत्या संयुज्यन्ते ब्रह्मचर्यमेव तद्यद्रात्रौ रत्या संयुज्यन्ते ॥
verses.iast: ahorātro vai prajāpatistasyāhareva prāṇo rātrirava rayiḥ prāṇaṁ vā ete praskandanti ye divā ratyā saṁyujyante brahmacaryameva tadyadrātrau ratyā saṁyujyante ||
verses.trans: "Day and night are Prajāpati. The day is prāṇa; the night is rayi. Those who unite sexually during the day squander their prāṇa. Therefore brahmacharya consists in uniting only at night."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara emphasises that prāṇa is the vital force that sustains spiritual progress — its wastage impedes the clarity needed for Self-knowledge. The regulation of sexual energy (brahmacharya in its graduated form, including householders) is not mere asceticism but a practical preservation of the vital force needed for meditation. For Śaṅkara, the deeper brahmacharya is celibacy of the mind — abiding in Brahman (brahmaṇi carati) at all times — but the physical regulation is the preparatory foundation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja understands this teaching in the context of dharma as divinely ordered living. The householder's brahmacharya — restricting sexuality to the night and to procreation — is part of the śāstrīya order that pleases the Lord. Unlike strict Advaita which views all householder life as a lesser stage, Rāmānuja's Viśiṣṭādvaita honours the gṛhastha āśrama as a legitimate vehicle for bhakti when lived according to dharma. This verse's teaching is thus integrated into a life of ordered devotion, not mere restraint.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva's tradition is equally clear: brahmacharya for the householder means restriction of sexual activity to night and to the purpose of procreation. The daytime belongs to prāṇa (divine energy) and should be devoted to worship, study, and service of Viṣṇu. Squandering prāṇa weakens the devotee's capacity for concentrated bhakti. In Madhva's ethical framework, bodily regulation and devotional practice are inseparable — a dissipated body cannot sustain the intense, focused love for Viṣṇu that leads to liberation.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo treats this verse as pointing to the conservation and transformation of vital energy. The sexual instinct, in the Integral Yoga framework, is not to be suppressed but transformed — its energy redirected toward the aspiration for divine union. The day as prāṇa and night as rayi reflects the diurnal rhythm of solar (consciousness) and lunar (vital) energies. The teaching on brahmacharya is preparatory; the ultimate goal is not restraint but the sublimation of the vital force into the flame of aspiration.

verses.dev: अन्नं वै प्रजापतिस्ततो ह वै तद्रेतस्ततः प्रजाः प्रजायन्ते ॥
verses.iast: annaṁ vai prajāpatistato ha vai tadretastatah prajāḥ prajāyante ||
verses.trans: "Food is Prajāpati. From food comes seed (retas); from seed, creatures are born."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara notes that this verse completes the chain of cosmic causation at the material level: Prajāpati → rayi → anna (food) → retas (seed) → prajāḥ (creatures). Food here is the material manifestation of rayi, the objective principle. Ultimately all of this is Brahman appearing as the material cause of the world. The Taittirīya Upaniṣad's teaching 'annaṁ brahma' (food is Brahman) resonates here — food is sacred because it is itself the objectified form of consciousness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees food as the Lord's own material body (acit) offered to sustain the Lord's conscious body (cit — the souls). The chain from food to seed to creatures is the chain of the Lord's own self-sustaining activity, maintaining His body-cosmos in continuous creative operation. Eating food is thus not merely a biological act but a participation in the Lord's own self-sustaining cosmic sacrifice, which is why the Bhagavad Gītā speaks of food as a sacred offering.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads anna (food) as Viṣṇu's material energy (māyā-śakti) manifesting as the nourishing basis of biological life. Seed and creatures are further expressions of Viṣṇu's creative power, which works through the material principle. The biological cycle of food→seed→life is a microcosm of the cosmic creative cycle that Viṣṇu sustains at every moment. No moment of biological life is independent of the divine will; even the most material processes are expressions of Viṣṇu's lordship.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees this verse as describing the descent of Spirit into its most dense material expression. Food (anna) is the Earth's gift — matter organised to support life. The chain food→seed→creatures is the evolutionary chain of increasing complexity, each level a more articulate expression of the hidden Spirit. In Aurobindo's system, the physical body nourished by food is the Earth's own body that Spirit has assumed — sacred, not to be despised, but to be transformed into a fit vehicle for the divine life.

verses.dev: तद्ये ह तत्प्रजापतिव्रतं चरन्ति ते मिथुनमुत्पादयन्ते तेषामेवैष ब्रह्मलोको येषां तपो ब्रह्मचर्यं येषु सत्यं प्रतिष्ठितम् ॥
verses.iast: tadye ha tatprajāpativrataṁ caranti te mithunamutpādayante teṣāmevaiṣa brahmaloко yeṣāṁ tapo brahmacaryaṁ yeṣu satyaṁ pratiṣṭhitam ||
verses.trans: "Those who observe the vow of Prajāpati produce a couple (progeny). For those in whom austerity, brahmacharya, and truth are firmly established — the world of Brahman belongs to them alone."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara distinguishes two levels of the Prajāpati vow: the householder level (producing righteous progeny) and the ascetic level (brahmacharya leading to brahmaloka and ultimate liberation). The world of Brahman (brahmaloka) is the highest state accessible through upāsanā — it leads to krama-mukti (liberation in stages), where even without immediate liberation the soul attains Brahman at the dissolution of the cosmos. Truth (satya) as the foundation suggests that all authentic spiritual practice is an alignment with the nature of Brahman itself, which is sat (being/truth).
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja notes that tapas, brahmacharya, and satya when combined with bhakti lead to the Lord's own abode (brahmaloka as Vaikuṇṭha). The vow of Prajāpati at the householder level produces righteous progeny who continue the dharmic lineage; at the ascetic level it produces liberation through devotion. Truth (satya) is particularly significant in Viśiṣṭādvaita: speaking and living the truth is an expression of one's fundamental alignment with the Lord, who is the ground of all reality.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva insists that brahmaloka here refers to Viṣṇu's supreme abode, Vaikuṇṭha, which is eternal and beyond the cyclic cosmos. The vow of Prajāpati observed in its highest form — complete brahmacharya, tapas, and satya dedicated to Viṣṇu — leads directly to the highest liberation (anuttama mokṣa). Those who produce progeny are fulfilling the lesser form of the vow, which is valuable but leads to lesser worlds. The hierarchy of goals is thus clear: progeny and temporal virtue below, eternal Viṣṇu-sāyujya above.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads this verse as affirming that two valid paths exist within the same comprehensive vision. Those who channel the life-force into creative partnership (mithuna — progeny) and those who conserve it for spiritual transformation (brahmacharya) both fulfill different aspects of the divine intention. The world of Brahman awaiting those of established tapas, brahmacharya, and truth is not a negation of the world of progeny but its spiritual fulfilment — the same Life-force that creates offspring can, when rightly directed, create the soul-offspring of spiritual realisation.

verses.dev: तेषामसौ विरजो ब्रह्मलोको न येषु जिह्ममनृतं न माया चेति ॥
verses.iast: teṣāmasau virajo brahmaloко na yeṣu jihmamanṛtaṁ na māyā ceti ||
verses.trans: "That pure, stainless world of Brahman belongs to those in whom there is no crookedness (jihma), no falsehood (anṛta), and no deceit (māyā)."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara sees the threefold freedom from crookedness, falsehood, and deceit as constituting the moral and psychological purity that makes the heart a fit vessel for brahma-jñāna. 'Virajam' (stainless) is a key Upaniṣadic word for liberation — the state beyond rajas (passion and agitation). The Advaita insight is that Brahman itself is viraja, and the practitioner who becomes viraja by removing taints recognises their identity with Brahman. Truth, straightforwardness, and non-deception are not mere virtues but the natural quality of a mind already touching its true nature.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja emphasises that the pure brahmaloka belongs to those whose bhakti is uncorrupted by crookedness (strategy in dealing with God and others), falsehood (misrepresentation of the Lord's nature), or deceit (performing devotion for ulterior motives). Pure devotion is selfless, transparent, and grounded in truth — exactly the disposition cultivated by the year of tapas, brahmacharya, and satya with which the Upaniṣad began. The stainless (viraja) world is the divine abode where such pure souls dwell eternally.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads this concluding verse of the first praśna as a stringent moral qualification for mokṣa: the liberated soul must be utterly free of crookedness, deception, and untruth. These are the very qualities that constitute adharmika existence — a life hostile to Viṣṇu's sovereign claim. The viraja brahmaloka is Vaikuṇṭha itself, where souls who have purified themselves of all these taints dwell in eternal, joyful service of Viṣṇu. No amount of ritual can compensate for a crooked or deceitful character.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo treats this verse as describing the soul's readiness for the supramental truth-world. Jihma (crookedness), anṛta (untruth), and māyā (deception) are the psychological signatures of the ego — the self that bends, falsifies, and conceals in its strategies of self-protection. The stainless brahmaloka is the Truth-consciousness where none of these distortions can exist. The Integral Yoga's cultivation of truthfulness (satya) at every level — thought, speech, action, intention — is the preparation for this pure world that the verse promises.

verses.dev: अथ हैनं भार्गवो वैदर्भिः पप्रच्छ भगवन् कति देवा देवत्वेनैनं विश्वं विधारयन्ते कतर एतत्प्रकाशयन्ते कः पुनरेषां वरिष्ठ इति ॥
verses.iast: atha hainaṁ bhārgavo vaidarbhiḥ papraccha bhagavan kati devā devatvenainaṁ viśvaṁ vidhārayante katara etatprakāśayante kaḥ punareṣāṁ variṣṭha iti ||
verses.trans: Then Bhārgava Vaidarbhi asked him: "Venerable sir, how many devas support this world by their divine power? How many reveal it? And which among them is the supreme one?"

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara notes that this question has three parts: the number of supporting devas, the number of illuminating devas, and the identity of the greatest. The answer will reveal that all these devas — fire, air, space, earth, water, sense organs, mind — are themselves dependent upon prāṇa, the principal vital force. From the Advaita standpoint, the entire inquiry leads toward the recognition that behind all deva-functions is the one consciousness (Ātman), which the text approaches through the doorway of prāṇa.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja interprets this question as seeking the inner hierarchy of the Lord's own manifested powers. The devas who support and illuminate the world are themselves the Lord's body — aspects of His cosmic form. The identification of prāṇa as the chief deva does not diminish the others but reveals the integrated structure of the Lord's cosmic self-expression. The 'greatest' deva ultimately points toward the Lord Himself who is the prāṇa-of-prāṇas (prāṇasya prāṇa).
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva holds that the devas (divine beings) who support and illuminate the cosmos are real, distinct entities — not mere metaphors. They act as agents of Viṣṇu, each governing a specific cosmic function. The question of which is 'greatest' (variṣṭha) will reveal prāṇa's functional supremacy among the devas, but this supremacy itself is derivative — prāṇa is supreme among devas because Viṣṇu has so ordained. The true Variṣṭha is always Viṣṇu alone.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees this question as the inquiry into the hierarchy of cosmic forces. The devas are not mythological persons but real principles of consciousness-force operating in the cosmic body. Their threefold function — supporting (dhāraṇa), illuminating (prakāśana), and the supremacy among them — maps onto Aurobindo's schema of existence-consciousness-force (sat-cit-śakti). Prāṇa emerging as the chief represents the discovery that Life is the organising principle between Matter below and Mind above.

verses.dev: तस्मै स होवाच आकाशो ह वा एष देवो वायुरग्निरापः पृथिवी वाङ्मनश्चक्षुः श्रोत्रं च ते प्रकाशयन्ते अभिविदन्ते वर्धन्ते च अथैतैस्त्वेव वर्षिष्ठः प्राण इति ॥
verses.iast: tasmai sa hovāca ākāśo ha vā eṣa devo vāyuragnirvāpaḥ pṛthivī vāṅmanaścakṣuḥ śrotraṁ ca te prakāśayante abhividante vardhante ca athaitaisteva variṣṭhaḥ prāṇa iti ||
verses.trans: He said to him: "Space (ākāśa), air (vāyu), fire (agni), water (āpaḥ), earth (pṛthivī), speech (vāk), mind (manas), sight (cakṣus), and hearing (śrotra) — these reveal and proclaim this world, and they exalt themselves. But the highest among all of them is prāṇa."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara notes that all nine listed forces — the five elements and four faculties — each claim supremacy in their respective domains. The resolution comes not through competition but through recognition: prāṇa is supreme not because it defeats the others but because all the others depend upon it. Remove prāṇa and every other function collapses. This points Advaitically toward Ātman: just as prāṇa is the basis of all the other devas, Ātman is the basis of prāṇa itself — the chain of dependency leads back to the one Self.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees the nine forces as the Lord's own energies (śaktis) differentiating themselves in the service of creation. The proclamation that prāṇa is supreme among them reflects the Lord's own ordering of His cosmic body. Prāṇa as the organising life-force holds together all other functions, much as the soul holds together the body — an image Rāmānuja uses for the relation of souls (cit) to God. The Lord is the prāṇa of all prāṇas, the ultimate organiser within and beyond this hierarchy.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva accepts the real plurality of these nine cosmic forces as distinct divine agents serving Viṣṇu. Prāṇa is proclaimed chief among the devas not by self-assertion but because Viṣṇu has granted it the highest functional authority in governing bodily life. The nine forces represent Viṣṇu's ninefold delegation of cosmic administration. Their proclamation (abhividante) is an acknowledgment of the divinely established cosmic order — not a competition among peers but a recognition of hierarchy.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo maps these nine forces onto the ascending hierarchy of planes: earth, water, fire, air, and space as the five material planes; speech, sight, hearing, and mind as the vital-mental faculties. Prāṇa is declared supreme because it is the bridge — the organising Life-power that links matter (the elements) with mind (the faculties). In the Integral view, Life is neither mere matter nor pure mind but a distinct and central cosmic principle without which neither material nor mental evolution is possible.

verses.dev: स एषोऽन्तरे भवति ते यदा सुप्तः स्वप्नं न विजानात्यथास्मिन्प्राणाग्नय एव समिद्यन्ते ॥
verses.iast: sa eṣo'ntare bhavati te yadā suptaḥ svapnaṁ na vijānātyathāsminprāṇāgnaya eva samidyante ||
verses.trans: "He (prāṇa) is in the intermediate (vital) region. When the person is in dreamless sleep, he does not perceive any dream — it is then that the fires of prāṇa alone blaze within him."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara reads this verse as evidence that prāṇa is independent of the mental and sensory faculties which fall dormant in deep sleep (suṣupti). When the organs of speech, sight, and hearing are withdrawn and even the dream-mind is quiescent, prāṇa alone continues to function. This points to prāṇa as the last support before the Ātman — in deep sleep all mental activity ceases, but prāṇa persists, keeping the body alive. The dissolution of sense-organs in deep sleep is a daily rehearsal of death, and prāṇa's persistence hints at the immortal self beneath it.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees deep sleep as the state in which the jīva (individual soul), having withdrawn from the outer world, rests close to the Lord who dwells in the heart. Prāṇa's continued activity in this state is the Lord's own life-sustaining grace operating even when the individual soul is unconscious. The fires of prāṇa (prāṇāgni) that blaze within the sleeping body are expressions of the Lord's perpetual sustenance — no moment of the soul's existence is without divine support.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that in deep sleep the jīva's cognitive powers are suspended but the body's vital fires continue — this is Viṣṇu's direct sustenance of the individual soul through prāṇa. The term 'prāṇāgnaya' (fires of prāṇa) is significant: prāṇa is identified with fire, an agent of Viṣṇu's cosmic energy. Even in the most inert state of existence, the Lord's energy (in the form of prāṇa) remains active, preventing the dissolution that would otherwise occur. This verse is a refutation of any notion that souls are self-sustaining.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads deep sleep (suṣupti) as the soul's nightly immersion in the inconscient — the return to the deeper levels of being where the mental construction dissolves. The persistence of the prāṇa-fires in this state shows that Life is deeper than mind — consciousness can withdraw from mental activity yet life continues. In the Integral framework, suṣupti is not mere unconsciousness but an entry into the subliminal self where the soul rests in the vital-matter nexus. The prāṇāgni burning in sleep is the cosmic Life-force maintaining its grip even when the surface mind lets go.

verses.dev: यथाग्रे राजा मामात्यानुद्युक्तान्ब्रूयाद्युष्मान् इत्थमेवाहं त्रैकावती ग्राम इत्थमेवैतान्प्रतितिष्ठध्वम् इत्येवमेव प्राणः स एव प्राणाणां श्रेष्ठोऽयमेव वरिष्ठः ॥
verses.iast: yathāgre rājā māmātyānudyuktānbrūyādyuṣmān itthamevāhaṁ traikāvatī grāma itthamevaitānpratitiṣṭhadhvam ityevameva prāṇaḥ sa eva prāṇāṇāṁ śreṣṭho'yameva variṣṭhaḥ ||
verses.trans: "Just as a king commands his ministers: 'You shall stay here, and you shall garrison that village' — in the same way prāṇa commands the other prāṇas (vital functions), each in its appointed place. Prāṇa is indeed the chief among prāṇas, the supreme."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara uses the king analogy to establish prāṇa's ruling authority. Just as ministers do nothing without the king's direction, no sense organ or vital function operates independently of prāṇa. The practical test of this supremacy will come in the next verses when prāṇa 'leaves' and all others are compelled to follow. From the Advaita perspective, this king-prāṇa analogy also suggests a further ruler behind the king — Ātman is the king of kings who commands even prāṇa.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja finds in the king analogy an image of divine governance. The Lord (the king) delegates authority to prāṇa (the prime minister) who in turn directs the individual vital functions. The hierarchy is thus: Lord → prāṇa → individual functions. This verse supports the Viśiṣṭādvaita vision of a cosmos governed by divine delegation — the Lord does not manage every microfunction directly but through a hierarchy of ordered powers, each serving as the instrument of the one above.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads the king as Viṣṇu, prāṇa as His chief executive in the domain of bodily life, and the individual prāṇas (apāna, vyāna, etc.) as Viṣṇu's subordinate cosmic servants. The military metaphor (garrisoning a village) reflects Madhva's strong sense of the cosmos as a domain of divine sovereignty where every entity has a divinely assigned post. Exceeding one's assigned function is disorder; performing it faithfully is dharma. Prāṇa's supremacy is its faithful execution of the Lord's command.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo finds the king analogy illuminating for the structure of the vital being. Prāṇa is the 'central vital' — the ruling will of the life-force that coordinates all the individual vital movements (the 'ministers'). In the Integral Yoga, the seeker must come to understand and govern this central vital, which otherwise acts as an autonomous king within the human system. When prāṇa is surrendered to the divine will, all the subordinate vital movements are also brought into alignment — the microcosmic parallel of the cosmic king's command.

verses.dev: तदु होवाच कोऽहमस्मीति तं ब्रूयाद्गर्हयन्तस्त्वामग्निरिव शाम्यति वायुरिव वाति आदित्य इव तपति वर्षतीन्द्र इव ॥
verses.iast: tadu hovāca ko'hamasmīti taṁ brūyādgarhayantastvāmagnirivaśāmyati vāyuriva vāti ādityaiva tapati varṣatīndra iva ||
verses.trans: "Prāṇa declared: 'Do not be deluded — I am this, I am this (prāṇa).' They did not believe it. Prāṇa then displayed its power: fire becomes cold, wind ceases to blow, the sun stops giving heat, rain does not fall — when prāṇa is displeased."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara notes the dramatic demonstration: when prāṇa is dishonoured, the entire cosmic structure begins to fail. Fire, wind, sun, and rain — the four great sustaining forces — all falter without prāṇa. This is not mythology but philosophical argument: prāṇa is the animating principle without which no material force can function. Advaitically, this illustrates that consciousness-life (prāṇa-cit) is prior to material function — matter without the vital principle is inert.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads this episode as a cosmic demonstration of the Lord's life-force (prāṇa) as the indispensable connector between divine will and material expression. When prāṇa withdraws its functioning, even the great devas — Agni, Vāyu, Āditya, Indra — cannot perform their cosmic offices. This shows that the Lord's grace, channeled through prāṇa, is what enables the cosmic devas to discharge their functions. Devotion to the Lord who is the source of prāṇa is thus the most fundamental act of cosmic alignment.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva sees this as a demonstration that all the great devas — Agni, Vāyu, Āditya, Indra — are absolutely dependent on Viṣṇu's prāṇa-śakti to function. When prāṇa withdraws, their entire power collapses. This reinforces the Dvaita hierarchy: no deva, however great, possesses inherent independent power. All cosmic function is a contingent gift from Viṣṇu, exercised through prāṇa. The other devas' initial disbelief in prāṇa's supremacy mirrors the ordinary soul's failure to recognise Viṣṇu's ultimate sovereignty.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo finds this verse a compelling image of the centrality of the Life-principle in all cosmic processes. When the Life-force withdraws — as in death — all material and energetic processes associated with that living system collapse. Fire, wind, sun, and rain as cosmic forces are all expressions of the universal prāṇa; remove the animating Life, and even the cosmic forces seem to lose their direction. This is the Upaniṣad's way of pointing to Life (prāṇa) as the crucial 'middle term' in the evolution from matter to consciousness.

verses.dev: अग्निरिवेदं सर्वं भस्मसाद्भवेत् यदा प्राण उत्क्रामति ततः सर्वाणि भूतान्यस्मात्प्रोत्क्रामन्ते प्राणे सन्ते सर्वाणि जीवन्ति ॥
verses.iast: agnirivedaṁ sarvaṁ bhasmasādbhaveta yadā prāṇa utkrāmati tataḥ sarvāṇi bhūtānyasmātprotkrāmante prāṇe sante sarvāṇi jīvanti ||
verses.trans: "When prāṇa departs, everything in this world would be reduced to ashes like (a world set on) fire. Then all beings depart from this world along with prāṇa. When prāṇa is content, all things live."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara uses this verse to establish prāṇa as the universal life-sustainer, analogous to how Ātman sustains prāṇa. The image of everything becoming ash when prāṇa departs is the Upaniṣadic equivalent of universal death — the dissolution of the phenomenal world when the animating principle is withdrawn. 'When prāṇa is content, all things live' points toward the spiritual teaching: just as bodily life depends on prāṇa, spiritual life (liberation) depends on the recognition of Ātman as prāṇasya prāṇa (the prāṇa of prāṇa).
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees the universal destruction threatened by prāṇa's departure as a cosmic image of pralaya (dissolution) — when the Lord withdraws His sustaining will, the entire world dissolves. Conversely, 'when prāṇa is content, all things live' is a statement of the Lord's perpetual grace in sustaining creation. The Lord's satisfaction (tushti) is the ground of cosmic existence. This verse implicitly teaches the dependence of all existence on divine goodwill — a foundation stone of the Viśiṣṭādvaita theology of grace.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads the universal collapse when prāṇa departs as an illustration of the total dependence of all entities — including the great devas — on Viṣṇu's prāṇa-śakti. The ash-like destruction parallels pralaya in Dvaita cosmology: the dissolution at the end of a cosmic cycle when Viṣṇu withdraws His sustaining energy. 'When prāṇa is content' refers to Viṣṇu's own contentment in sustaining creation — a state of divine self-enjoyment (ānanda) through which all lesser lives are upheld.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads the cosmic fire-dissolution as an image of what happens when the Life-force is withdrawn from a system — it becomes entropy, ash, inert matter without structure or purpose. The second statement — 'when prāṇa is content, all things live' — points to the universal satisfaction of the Life-power as the precondition for all existence, growth, and evolution. In Aurobindo's vision, the Spirit (sat) descends into prāṇa (life-force) to sustain matter (annam) in its evolutionary journey; cosmic peace is the contentment of that universal Life-Spirit in its self-expression.

verses.dev: तदेतत्श्लोकभिरुक्तम् ॥ यथा क्रौञ्चस्य पक्षाणि संपतन्त्यस्य सर्वशः एवं सर्वे देवाः सर्वाणि भूतानि प्राण एव ॥
verses.iast: tadetatshlokabhiruktam || yathā krauñcasya pakṣāṇi saṁpatantyasya sarvaśaḥ evaṁ sarve devāḥ sarvāṇi bhūtāni prāṇa eva ||
verses.trans: "This is expressed in the following verse: 'Just as all the feathers of a crane (krauñca bird) converge on the central quill — in the same way, all the devas and all beings are gathered in prāṇa alone.'"

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara praises this verse's poetic clarity: the crane's feathers as a single, beautifully structured wing converging on the central spine is an image of the many converging in the one. All devas and all beings 'gather in prāṇa' as their common centre. The verse anticipates the Muṇḍaka Upaniṣad's image of sparks from fire — but here the convergence is emphasised over the divergence. The direction of the teaching is: from many to one, from deva-plurality to prāṇa-unity, from prāṇa to Ātman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees all the devas as converging in prāṇa just as the Lord's multiple energies (śaktis) are always unified in His single divine will. The image of convergence supports Viśiṣṭādvaita's vision of divine unity-in-diversity: the Lord's body (the cosmos) is complex and multiform, but all its parts converge on and find their coherence in the Lord's central self. The crane-wing as an integrated whole is an apt image of the Lord's body (sarīra) — many parts, one organism.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva appreciates this simile as an image of hierarchical unity: the many subordinate to the one. All devas and beings converge in prāṇa as all of Viṣṇu's cosmic agents converge in His will. The crane's feathers do not lose their distinctness — they remain real individual feathers — but they all depend on the central quill to form a wing. So too, all jīvas and devas remain really distinct yet all depend on Viṣṇu (through prāṇa as His instrument). Unity without the loss of distinction is the Dvaita reading.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo finds the crane-wing image beautiful for expressing the unity of the Life-principle. All forms of life — from the divine to the material — are expressions of the one prāṇa. In Aurobindo's integral view, evolution is the progressive revelation of this unity: at first the various 'feathers' of life seem unrelated, but as consciousness evolves they are seen as aspects of one integrated Life-Spirit. The bird itself — the complete organism with all its feathers unified — is the image of the supramentally organised cosmos.

verses.dev: प्राणो ग्निः प्राणो रविः प्राणो वर्षः प्राणो वासवः प्राणः पृथिवी रायिर्देवी विश्वस्य जगतः ॥
verses.iast: prāṇo'gniḥ prāṇo raviḥ prāṇo varṣaḥ prāṇo vāsavaḥ prāṇaḥ pṛthivī rāyirdevī viśvasya jagataḥ ||
verses.trans: "Prāṇa is fire; prāṇa is the sun; prāṇa is rain; prāṇa is Vāsava (Indra); prāṇa is earth; prāṇa is matter (rayi) — prāṇa is the divine (devī) in the whole world."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara reads this litany of identifications as the Upaniṣad's way of establishing prāṇa's comprehensive cosmic identity — fire, sun, rain, Indra, earth, and even rayi (matter) are all prāṇa. This is the sarvātmabhāva (the vision of the Self in all) at the prāṇa level. From the Advaita perspective, these identifications are ultimately statements about Brahman: since prāṇa is ultimately Brahman, and fire, sun, earth etc. are all prāṇa, all phenomenal reality is ultimately Brahman alone.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees this verse as a hymn to the Lord's pervasive presence through prāṇa. Fire, sun, rain, Indra, earth — each of these is the Lord's own body animated by His prāṇa. The inclusion of rayi (matter) alongside the divine (devī) shows that both the spiritual and the material are equally the Lord's self-expression. This prevents any Viśiṣṭādvaita dualism: matter is not alien to the divine but its own body, equally 'divine' in the sense of being the Lord's self-manifestation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets these identifications not as ontological equations but as expressions of Viṣṇu's pervasive śakti working through each cosmic agent. 'Prāṇo'gniḥ' does not mean prāṇa literally is fire, but that Viṣṇu's prāṇa-energy is what makes Agni function as fire. Each cosmic force (sun, rain, Indra, earth) operates through Viṣṇu's prāṇa as His instrument. Rayi as devī (the divine feminine) completes the picture: Viṣṇu's material energy (prakṛti) is also divine because it is Viṣṇu's own śakti.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads this as a vision of the cosmic prāṇa-śakti expressing itself in all the forms of nature — fire, sun, rain, wind, earth, and matter. Prāṇa is the universal Energy (śakti) that takes all forms. The identification of prāṇa with both the spiritual (Indra, fire, sun) and the material (earth, rayi) is precisely the integral vision: Life is the nexus where spirit and matter meet, and the universal prāṇa pervades both poles. 'Devī of the whole world' points to the Mother-Shakti that Aurobindo's Integral Yoga reveres as the cosmic creative Force.

verses.dev: स एष नेता ससृजानः प्रजापतिरिन्द्रश्च राजा जायते ॥
verses.iast: sa eṣa netā sasṛjānaḥ prajāpatirindraśca rājā jāyate ||
verses.trans: "He (prāṇa), the guide and the creator, arises as Prajāpati and also as Indra the king."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara takes 'netā' (guide) and 'sasṛjāna' (creator) as indicating prāṇa's cosmic roles. Prāṇa as Prajāpati is the creative cosmic Self; prāṇa as Indra is the ruling principle. These two — creation and governance — belong to prāṇa because prāṇa is the manifestation of Ātman's creative intelligence in the phenomenal world. The equation prāṇa = Prajāpati = Indra collapses the usual distinction between creator and ruler, suggesting that the same Ātman performs all cosmic functions under different names.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads this as the Lord (Viṣṇu) manifesting His creative and kingly aspects through prāṇa. Prajāpati is the Lord's creative aspect (sṛṣṭi-kāra); Indra is the Lord's regal, protective aspect. Both are manifestations of the one Lord's infinite nature. The identification of prāṇa with both affirms that the Lord pervades the entire hierarchy of cosmic governance from creation to kingship. The devotee who knows this worships the Lord in all cosmic roles simultaneously.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva holds that Prajāpati (Brahma) and Indra are real, distinct divine beings, but both operate as Viṣṇu's agents empowered by prāṇa-śakti. Prāṇa 'arises as' Prajāpati and Indra means Viṣṇu's life-force manifests through these functionaries. They are not illusory but genuinely distinct entities with genuinely different roles, all receiving their power from the same divine source. This avoids both the Advaita collapse into oneness and any notion of independent divine plurality.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees prāṇa-as-Prajāpati as the cosmic creative Energy (śakti) that generates existence, and prāṇa-as-Indra as the mental-vital ruling principle that governs the created world. In the Integral scheme, the transition from Prajāpati (the creator) to Indra (the king) represents the evolution from the vital-creative level to the mental-governing level of consciousness. Prāṇa underlies both, being the Energy that first creates and then organises what it has created.

verses.dev: स एतान् देवानसृजत सोऽयं द्वितीयः प्राणः प्रजापतिर्मृत्युः सर्वं विद्वान्मृत्युः प्राणः ॥
verses.iast: sa etān devānasṛjat so'yaṁ dvitīyaḥ prāṇaḥ prajāpatirmṛtyuḥ sarvaṁ vidvānmṛtyuḥ prāṇaḥ ||
verses.trans: "He (prāṇa) created these devas. He is Prajāpati; he is death (mṛtyu); he is prāṇa again — the all-knowing death, the prāṇa."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara points out the striking identification of prāṇa with both Prajāpati (the creator-lord) and mṛtyu (death). This apparent paradox resolves in Advaita: the same power that creates also destroys — creation and destruction are two aspects of the one māyā-śakti playing on the surface of Brahman. 'All-knowing death' (sarvaṁ vidvān mṛtyuḥ) points toward the Kena Upaniṣad's teaching: what you think you know, you don't know; what knows even your knowing — that is Brahman-death, the dissolution of the illusory knower.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees prāṇa as Prajāpati (the Lord's creative power) and simultaneously as death — a recognition that the Lord is both creator and final receiver of all creation. In Viśiṣṭādvaita, the Lord is the sṛṣṭi-kāraka (creator), sthiti-kāraka (sustainer), and saṁhāra-kāraka (dissolver). The identification of prāṇa with all three roles — creation, knowledge, and death — is a compressed statement of the Lord's comprehensive sovereignty over all temporal processes. The devotee accepts this with equanimity, trusting the Lord's wisdom in both giving and taking life.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that prāṇa here is identified with 'all-knowing death' — a reference to Yama (the lord of death) who is also an agent of Viṣṇu. Death is not the negation of divine governance but its enforcement — Viṣṇu, through prāṇa and Yama, ensures that karma is appropriately distributed across lives. The 'all-knowing' quality of death (sarvaṁ vidvān) indicates Viṣṇu's omniscience operating through the process of death and rebirth to bring justice to every jīva.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads this identification of prāṇa with death as a profound insight: Life and Death are not opposites but aspects of the same Life-force. Death is the prāṇa returning to its source, gathering back the energy that was extended in creation. In Aurobindo's Integral framework, physical death is not the final word — the Life-force continues, evolving through successive forms. 'All-knowing death' is the wisdom-aspect of the Life-force that knows when a particular form has fulfilled its evolutionary purpose and must be dissolved for a newer, higher expression.

verses.dev: त्रयः प्राणा अयमन्तःशरीरे जठरोऽग्निः सोऽयं पक्त्या जठरस्थाने धमति ॥
verses.iast: trayaḥ prāṇā ayamantaḥśarīre jaṭharo'gniḥ so'yaṁ paktyā jaṭharasthāne dhamati ||
verses.trans: "The three prāṇas within the body — the fire within the stomach (jaṭharāgni) blows (digests) by its cooking power in the place of the stomach."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara connects the threefold prāṇa to the digestive fire (jaṭharāgni) which is the internal manifestation of the cosmic fire (Agni) identified earlier with prāṇa. This establishes the micro-macro correspondence: the fire of digestion within every body is the same prāṇa that shines as the sun externally. The cooking (pakti) function is not merely biological but cosmic — it is the universal transformation principle (Agni-prāṇa) working at the individual scale.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees the digestive fire (jaṭharāgni) as the Lord's own prāṇa sustaining the individual body. The Bhagavad Gītā verse 'ahaṁ vaiśvānaro bhūtvā prāṇināṁ dehamāśritaḥ / prāṇāpānasamāyuktaḥ pacāmyannaṁ caturvidham' (15.14) directly supports this: the Lord Himself is the digestive fire within every living being. Every act of digestion is thus a form of divine action within the body, the Lord's intimate sustenance of the soul He inhabits.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads jaṭharāgni as Viṣṇu's appointed agent of digestion — a divine fire functioning in the body under Viṣṇu's direction. The threefold prāṇa (prāṇa, apāna, samāna) each has a distinct role in digestion and metabolism. This reflects Viṣṇu's meticulous governance of the body's interior life. No bodily function is mere biology — each is an instance of divine governance at the physiological level. To eat mindfully, dedicating one's food to Viṣṇu, is to participate consciously in this divine act.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees the digestive fire as the symbol of transformation — the central capacity of the vital body to take in, process, and assimilate experience. Just as jaṭharāgni transforms raw food into usable energy, the psychological-vital being must transform raw life-experience into wisdom and spiritual force. In the Integral Yoga, the transformation of the vital being (prāṇamaya kośa) requires exactly this 'digestive' capacity — the ability to take in all experience, including suffering, and transform it into fuel for spiritual growth.

verses.dev: अपानो जठराग्नौ समिद्धे वायौ प्राणः सममृच्छति उद्दानस्तु समानः शमन्ति सर्वे प्राणाः ॥
verses.iast: apāno jaṭharāgnau samiddhe vāyau prāṇaḥ samamṛcchati uddānasthu samānaḥ śamanti sarve prāṇāḥ ||
verses.trans: "When the gastric fire (jaṭharāgni) is kindled by apāna (the downward breath), prāṇa (the upward breath) joins with it evenly. Udāna, being the samāna, pacifies them all — all the prāṇas come to rest."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara describes the interplay of the five prāṇas in the digestive process: apāna fans the digestive fire, prāṇa carries food downward, samāna distributes nutrients evenly, and udāna is the outcome — the rising vital energy that results from successful digestion. The pacification of all prāṇas (śamanti) points toward the meditative state where the prāṇas are stilled in samādhi — just as digestion, when complete, brings a natural quiet to the vital forces.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads this physiological description as the Lord's own inner governance of the body's vital economy. The Lord as antaryāmin (inner ruler) coordinates the five prāṇas with perfect precision, maintaining the body's homeostasis. The mutual balancing described here — apāna and prāṇa meeting in the digestive fire, samāna harmonising, udāna integrating — is a microcosm of the divine governance that maintains cosmic balance (ṛta) at every scale.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva sees the five prāṇas as five distinct divine servants of Viṣṇu operating within the body. Their coordination in the digestive process is not automatic but is Viṣṇu's perpetual directive will operating through them. When digestion is complete ('śamanti sarve prāṇāḥ'), it is because Viṣṇu has allowed the digestive process to reach its natural completion. Bodily health is thus a form of divine grace, and its disruption is a sign that karma or some imbalance has interrupted the Lord's smooth governance.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees in the five prāṇas an anatomy of the vital being that maps onto his own analysis of the vital plane. Apāna as the root-vital force, prāṇa as the central vital, samāna as the harmonising principle, udāna as the aspiring vital, and vyāna as the extending vital — together they constitute the full economy of the vital body. When all prāṇas 'come to rest' (śamanti), it corresponds to the state of vital peace (vital nirvana) that Aurobindo describes as the foundation for deeper supramental opening.

verses.dev: एष उ ह वा एतद्वेद न हास्य प्रजा जायते यो वेद प्राणस्य प्राणं नयनस्य नयनं श्रोत्रस्य श्रोत्रमन्नस्यान्नं मनसो मनः ॥
verses.iast: eṣa u ha vā etadveda na hāsya prajā jāyate yo veda prāṇasya prāṇaṁ nayanasya nayanaṁ śrotrasya śrotramAnnasyānnaṁ manaso manaḥ ||
verses.trans: "Truly, he who knows this knows the prāṇa of prāṇa, the sight of sight, the hearing of hearing, the food of food, the mind of mind. He verily knows Brahman the ancient. No son is born to him (he need not wish for progeny)."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara recognises this as a direct echo of the Kena Upaniṣad's language: 'the eye of the eye, the ear of the ear, the mind of mind.' The 'prāṇa of prāṇa' is that which animates even prāṇa — i.e., Ātman/Brahman. The knowledge described is not conceptual but identificatory: knowing Brahman as the ground of prāṇa, sight, hearing, food, and mind. 'No son is born to him' means he transcends the desire for progeny because he has found the immortal Self — children provide symbolic immortality, but the knower of Brahman is already immortal.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads 'prāṇa of prāṇa' as the Lord who is the inner life of prāṇa itself — the antaryāmin who stands behind every vital function. He who knows this knows the Lord directly, and such knowledge is the highest — surpassing the Kena-style via negativa by adding the personal, devotional dimension. 'No son is born to him' indicates that he has found a relationship with the eternal Lord that surpasses all temporal relationships. He is freed from the saṁsāric desire for biological immortality through offspring.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva identifies 'prāṇa of prāṇa' as Viṣṇu, who is the source and sustainer of prāṇa. He who knows Viṣṇu as the ultimate ground of all the faculties — prāṇa, sight, hearing, food, mind — knows the 'ancient Brahman' (pūrvam brahma). The statement 'no son is born to him' has a double meaning in Dvaita: he is freed from the saṁsāric desire for sons (since he has found eternal life in Viṣṇu), and also, being fully devoted to Viṣṇu, he no longer needs the householder's path of progeny for spiritual fulfillment.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads 'prāṇa of prāṇa' as the Supermind — the consciousness-truth that underlies and powers all the faculties. Just as prāṇa is the life of the senses, the Supermind is the truth-life of prāṇa. He who knows this knows the ancient Brahman — the original, pre-temporal Ground from which all evolution proceeds. 'No son is born to him' means he has transcended the biological level of existence — not that he rejects life, but that he has found the source of all life and is no longer dependent on its biological expressions for meaning or continuity.

verses.sec: Praśna 3 — The Origin and Functions of Prāṇa
verses.dev: अथ हैनं कौसल्यश्चाश्वलायनः पप्रच्छ भगवन्कुत एष प्राणो जायते कथमायात्यस्मिञ्शरीर आत्मानं वा प्रविभज्य कथं प्रतिष्ठते केनोत्क्रामति कथं बाह्यमभिधत्ते कथमध्यात्मं चेति ॥
verses.iast: atha hainaṁ kausalyaścāśvalāyanaḥ papraccha bhagavankuta eṣa prāṇo jāyate kathamāyātyasmiñśarīra ātmānaṁ vā pravibhajya kathaṁ pratiṣṭhate kenotkrāmati kathaṁ bāhyamabhidhatte kathamadhyātmaṁ ceti ||
verses.trans: Then Kausalya Āśvalāyana asked him: "Venerable sir, from where is prāṇa born? How does it come into this body? How does it divide itself and establish itself? By what does it depart? How does it relate to the external world, and how to the individual self (adhyātma)?"

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara praises this question as the most comprehensive and philosophically precise of all the praśnas. Kausalya asks about prāṇa's origin, mode of entry, manner of division, cause of departure, and its double relationship to cosmos and individual. These six questions trace prāṇa's entire lifecycle and reveal its true nature. For Advaita, answering these questions will ultimately show that prāṇa arises from Ātman, abides in Ātman, and returns to Ātman — meaning the entire cycle of prāṇa is Brahman's own self-expression.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees this question as seeking the full theology of the Lord's prāṇa-śakti: its divine origin, its mode of operation within individual bodies, its distribution in the cosmic and individual dimensions, and its ultimate return. Understanding prāṇa in all these dimensions is understanding the Lord's sustaining grace in its complete expression. The question 'adhyātmam' and 'bāhyam' (external and internal) reflects the dual scale of divine operations that characterises all Vedāntic cosmology.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that Kausalya's six-part question reflects the complete range of prāṇa's operations as Viṣṇu's agent. The questions about origin, entry, division, departure, external, and internal dimensions all have specific answers that reveal the detailed architecture of Viṣṇu's governance of life. Unlike Advaita, which sees prāṇa as ultimately collapsing into the non-dual Ātman, Madhva sees each question as having a genuinely distinct answer, reflecting the real plurality of divine functions.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo finds this the most integral of all the questions: it seeks to understand prāṇa (the Life-force) in all its dimensions — cosmological, ontological, physiological, individual, and universal. This is the question of someone who wants not merely liberation but comprehensive understanding. In the Integral Yoga framework, understanding the Life-force in all its operations is essential — not to transcend it but to transform it. Kausalya's question is the question of the integral seeker who wants wholeness, not mere escape.

verses.sec: Praśna 3 — The Origin and Functions of Prāṇa
verses.dev: तस्मै स होवाच अतिप्रश्नान्पृच्छसि ब्रह्मिष्ठोऽसीति तस्मात्तेऽहं ब्रवीमि ॥
verses.iast: tasmai sa hovāca atipraśnānpṛcchasi brahmiṣṭho'sīti tasmātte'haṁ bravīmi ||
verses.trans: "He (Pippalāda) said to him: 'You ask very deep questions. You are the most devoted to Brahman among us — therefore I will tell you.'"
