commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara notes that Pippalāda's praise of Kausalya as 'most devoted to Brahman' (brahmiṣṭha) indicates that the profundity of one's question reflects the depth of one's spiritual development. The questions are not merely intellectual but arise from genuine brahma-jijñāsā — the burning desire to know Brahman. Pippalāda's willingness to give a full answer is the teacher's gift to a qualified pupil — the transmission of jñāna is possible only when the student is ready.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads Pippalāda's praise as the recognition that Kausalya's deep devotion to Brahman has prepared him for the deepest teaching. In Viśiṣṭādvaita, the guru's grace is as essential as the student's preparation — Pippalāda's 'therefore I will tell you' is the moment of grace, the teacher's decision to open the highest teaching. The qualification 'brahmiṣṭha' reflects Rāmānuja's insistence that true knowledge of Brahman requires not just intellectual preparation but loving devotion to the Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva sees this moment as the exemplary guru-śiṣya relationship: the student who asks with genuine devotion receives the fullest teaching. Kausalya's being the 'most devoted to Brahman' among the students means he is most devoted to Viṣṇu — and therefore most deserving of the highest revelation. In Dvaita pedagogy, the teacher does not withhold truth from the qualified — withholding wisdom from those capable of receiving it is a failure of duty. Pippalāda fulfills his duty with wholehearted generosity.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads this exchange as a model of the integral method of transmission. The question reveals the depth of Kausalya's aspiration — not merely intellectual but ontological, seeking to know the Life-force in all its dimensions. Pippalāda recognises this aspiration as the mark of the highest seeker (brahmiṣṭha). The guru's response 'therefore I will tell you' is the moment when the cosmic knowledge, held by the realised teacher, finds its way into the aspiring disciple through the bridge of qualified receptivity.

verses.sec: Praśna 3 — The Origin and Functions of Prāṇa
verses.dev: आत्मन एष प्राणो जायते यथास्मिन्पुरुषे छायैतस्मिन्नेतदाततं मनोकृतेनायात्यस्मिञ्शरीरे ॥
verses.iast: ātmana eṣa prāṇo jāyate yathāsminpuruṣe chāyaitasminneta dātatam manokṛtenāyātyasmiñśarīre ||
verses.trans: "Prāṇa is born from the Self (Ātman). Just as a shadow (follows) a person, so is prāṇa spread out in this (Ātman). Prāṇa comes into this body through the action of the mind (manokṛtena)."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara highlights the key statement: prāṇa is born from Ātman (ātmana eṣa prāṇo jāyate). This establishes the metaphysical hierarchy: Ātman is primary; prāṇa is secondary, dependent, derivative. The shadow analogy is precise — a shadow has no independent existence, arising and moving entirely in dependence on the person casting it. So prāṇa has no self-standing reality; it is the 'shadow' of Ātman appearing in the phenomenal world. The role of the mind (manas) in drawing prāṇa into the body connects this teaching to the doctrine of rebirth — it is accumulated desires (saṃskāras) in the mind that pull prāṇa into a new body.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees prāṇa arising from the Lord's own Ātman — the supreme Self from whose infinite fullness all cosmic functions derive. The shadow image expresses the Lord's pervasiveness: prāṇa is 'spread out' in the Ātman as a shadow is spread out from its source, never separable from it. 'Manokṛtena' (through the mind's action) indicates that the individual soul's desires and past karma draw prāṇa into a particular body — a process the Lord permits in accordance with each soul's karmic trajectory.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva insists that 'Ātman' here refers to Paramātman (Viṣṇu), not jīvātman. Prāṇa is born from Viṣṇu's will and pervades the individual body as Viṣṇu's śakti. The shadow analogy shows prāṇa's complete dependence on Viṣṇu — without the Lord, prāṇa has no existence, just as a shadow has no existence without the object casting it. 'Manokṛtena' refers to the jīva's karma operating through its mind, which Viṣṇu allows to draw prāṇa into a corresponding body.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads 'Ātman' here as the supramental or higher Self — the luminous source from which the Life-force descends. Prāṇa as the shadow of Ātman is a profound image: shadow is the same substance as the object, but seen under different light conditions. The Life-force is Spirit seen in the reduced light of material consciousness. 'Through the action of mind' (manokṛtena) indicates that consciousness (even as the subtle mind-principle) is the mechanism through which Life descends into matter — a key point in Aurobindo's evolutionary schema.

verses.sec: Praśna 3 — The Origin and Functions of Prāṇa
verses.dev: यथा राजा एवमेवायमात्मा इमान् प्राणान् पृथक् पृथगेव संनिदधाति स एष वैश्वानरो विश्वरूपः प्राणोऽग्निरुदयते ॥
verses.iast: yathā rājā evamevāyamātmā imān prāṇān pṛthak pṛthageva saṁnidadhāti sa eṣa vaiśvānaro viśvarūpaḥ prāṇo'gnirудayate ||
verses.trans: "Just as a king commands officers, so this Self (Ātman) assigns these prāṇas each to their separate place. He — the universal fire (Vaiśvānara), of universal form — rises as prāṇa and fire."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara uses the king analogy (repeated from Praśna 2) at a deeper level: now it is the Ātman, not just prāṇa, who assigns the vital functions to their places. The Ātman commands prāṇa as the king commands his officers. This hierarchical image directly establishes Ātman's supremacy over prāṇa, completing the chain: Brahman → Ātman → prāṇa → bodily functions. The identification with Vaiśvānara ties the individual body's vital fire back to the cosmic fire of the first praśna.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees the Ātman-as-king assigning prāṇas to their functions as the Lord governing His own body (the cosmic and individual organism) through the hierarchy of His prāṇa-śakti. The image of a king assigning officials to their posts is a favourite Rāmānuja analogy: the Lord is the supreme sovereign (sarva-śāsin) whose will governs every cosmic and individual function with perfect order and justice. Vaiśvānara as the manifestation of this divine will in fire affirms the sanctity of the digestive and cosmic fire.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads 'Ātman' here as Viṣṇu (Paramātman), who assigns the five prāṇas to their specific positions in the body with sovereign authority. The king analogy reinforces the Dvaita hierarchy: Viṣṇu as the absolute king, prāṇas as His provincial governors, and the bodily functions as the administered regions. Vaiśvānara rising as prāṇa and fire shows that even the universal fire is Viṣṇu's appointed agent — not a co-equal power but a commissioned officer.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads this verse as describing the organising role of the higher Self in the vital economy. The 'king' (Ātman) assigns each vital function its domain, creating the ordered differentiation that makes a living organism possible. In Aurobindo's terms, this is the causal Supermind organising the vital-physical being through its delegated prāṇa-forces. The identification with Vaiśvānara shows that this organisation is ultimately one of cosmic fire — the creative, transformative energy of consciousness operating at every scale.

verses.sec: Praśna 3 — The Origin and Functions of Prāṇa
verses.dev: अपानं गुदोपस्थे प्रतिष्ठापयति चक्षुःश्रोत्रे मुखनासिकाभ्यां प्राणः स्वयं प्रातिष्ठते मध्ये तु समानः ॥
verses.iast: apānaṁ gudopasthe pratiṣṭhāpayati cakṣuḥśrotre mukhanāsikābhyāṁ prāṇaḥ svayaṁ prātiṣṭhate madhye tu samānaḥ ||
verses.trans: "Apāna is established in the organs of excretion and generation. Prāṇa (the upward breath) establishes itself in the eyes, ears, mouth, and nose. In the middle is samāna."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara describes the precise anatomy of the five prāṇas: apāna governs the lower region (elimination and generation), prāṇa governs the upper region (sensory perception and respiration), and samāna occupies the middle (digestion and distribution). This triadic division — lower, middle, upper — mirrors the cosmic tripartition of earth (rayi), atmosphere, and heaven (prāṇa-sun) in Praśna 1. The body is a microcosm of the cosmos, governed by the same principles.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja uses this physiological division to illustrate the Lord's intimate governance of every bodily region. The Lord as antaryāmin operates through apāna in the lower body, through prāṇa in the upper, and through samāna in the middle — three distinct divine operations within one body. No part of the body is outside divine governance. This supports the Viśiṣṭādvaita vision of the body as entirely the Lord's domain, not a region of profane existence separate from the divine.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva accepts this anatomical division as a real description of Viṣṇu's differentiated life-governance within the body. Apāna, prāṇa, and samāna are three distinct vital agents, each with a divinely specified domain. Their precise allocation reflects Viṣṇu's perfect wisdom in designing the body's functional architecture. The medical implications are also real for Madhva: understanding the prāṇas' functions enables right living that honours Viṣṇu's bodily design.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads the threefold division as the body's vital economy: apāna as the root-vital (connected to earth and the material base), prāṇa as the central-vital (connected to the senses and aspiration), and samāna as the harmonising-vital (connected to integration and distribution). In the Integral Yoga, the transformation of the vital being requires work in all three regions: the root must be purified, the senses redirected toward the divine, and the integrating samāna must be filled with the light of higher consciousness.

verses.sec: Praśna 3 — The Origin and Functions of Prāṇa
verses.dev: स एष समानः समानस्य दाने चाप्याने च समानः सपत्नेष्वपि समानो भवति उदानो वा एष उत्क्रामति ॥
verses.iast: sa eṣa samānaḥ samānasya dāne cāpyāne ca samānaḥ sapaneṣvapi samāno bhavati udāno vā eṣa utkrāmati ||
verses.trans: "This samāna (the equalising breath), distributing food equally to all organs of the body, is the same in all. Udāna, verily, carries the soul upward."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara explains samāna as the prāṇa that distributes the essence of digested food equally (sama) to all parts of the body — it is the equaliser, the harmoniser, the integrator. Udāna's upward movement at death (utkrāmati) is the vital force that carries the subtle body out of the physical — a key principle in Vedāntic eschatology. The one who dies with good karma, through udāna, rises to higher worlds; the one with bad karma sinks. The direction of udāna at death reflects the quality of one's life.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees samāna as the Lord's equalising grace within the body — distributing nourishment without favouritism to every organ. This is a bodily expression of the Lord's impartial sustenance of all beings (samatvam). Udāna carrying the soul upward at death is the Lord's directing grace that guides the departing soul toward the realm appropriate to its devotion. For the true bhakta, udāna carries the soul toward the Lord's own abode; for others, it takes them to appropriate intermediate worlds.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes samāna's equalising function as Viṣṇu's mechanism for maintaining bodily homeostasis — a reflection of Viṣṇu's perfect justice (sama) in administering all affairs. Udāna's role at death is crucial in Madhva's eschatology: it is the vital force that Viṣṇu uses to carry the jīva out of the body and toward its karmic destination. For the devotee of Viṣṇu, udāna carries the soul directly to Vaikuṇṭha; for others, it channels the soul through the appropriate cosmic realms.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads samāna as the vital force of equalisation and integration — the principle that maintains balance and coherence in a living system. In the psychology of the Integral Yoga, samāna corresponds to what Aurobindo calls 'the equalised vital' — the vital energy that has become calm, distributing itself evenly rather than rushing preferentially toward objects of desire. Udāna as the ascending force at death points to the soul's upward aspiration — the evolutionary impulse that carries consciousness toward higher levels of manifestation.

verses.sec: Praśna 3 — The Origin and Functions of Prāṇa
verses.dev: उदानः पुण्यः पुण्यलोकमेव नयत्यपुण्येन पापलोकमुभाभ्यामेव मनुष्यलोकं मनुष्यलोकमेव ॥
verses.iast: udānaḥ puṇyaḥ puṇyalokaṁ nayatyapuṇyena pāpalokaṁ ubhābhyāmeva manuṣyalokaṁ manuṣyalokaṁ eva ||
verses.trans: "Through virtue, udāna leads to the virtuous worlds; through sin, to the sinful worlds; through a mixture of both, to the world of men."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara connects this to the law of karma operating through udāna at death. The upward-carrying vital force directs the subtle body according to the predominant disposition accumulated during life. This is not arbitrary but strictly correlative — karma shapes the direction of udāna, which determines the next rebirth. For Advaita, this entire mechanism is the working of avidyā (ignorance) generating saṃsāra; the liberated one who has known Brahman transcends this mechanism entirely, for udāna carries nothing when the ego-knot has been dissolved.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads this as the Lord's perfect moral governance operating through udāna: karma is not an impersonal mechanism but the Lord's own justice-principle (dharma-śakti) working through the prāṇa-system. The three destinations — virtuous worlds, sinful worlds, the human world — reflect the Lord's precise calibration of consequence to action. For the devotee who has surrendered to the Lord (prapanna), this entire mechanism of karma-karma-phala (action-result) is overridden by divine grace.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva sees this verse as a foundational statement of Dvaita ethics and eschatology. Viṣṇu's justice operates through udāna to send souls exactly where their karma merits — to higher worlds (svarga) for the virtuous, to lower worlds (naraka) for the sinful, or back to human existence for the mixed. This precise allocation is not mechanical fate but Viṣṇu's personal governance — He knows every soul's karma and directs udāna accordingly. Only through devotion to Viṣṇu does the soul escape this karmic cycle entirely.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees this verse as describing the psychic law of consequence — the soul goes where its accumulated consciousness-tendency directs it. Virtue creates a luminous, aspiring vital condition that udāna naturally carries upward; sin creates a dense, downward-pulling vital condition. The mixed soul returns to the human sphere to continue its evolution. In Aurobindo's vision, the human world is itself the sphere of evolution — the most important of the three destinations, for it alone offers the possibility of conscious self-transcendence and supramental transformation.

verses.sec: Praśna 3 — The Origin and Functions of Prāṇa
verses.dev: आदित्यो ह वै बाह्यः प्राण उदयत्येष ह्येनं चाक्षुषं प्राणमनुगृह्णानः पृथिव्यां या देवता सैषा पुरुषस्याप्यानं अन्तरिक्षं समानः ॥
verses.iast: ādityo ha vai bāhyaḥ prāṇa udayatyeṣa hyenaṁ cākṣuṣaṁ prāṇamanugṛhṇānaḥ pṛthivyāṁ yā devatā saiṣā puruṣasyāpyānaṁ antarikaṁ samānaḥ ||
verses.trans: "The sun is indeed the external prāṇa. It rises, graciously sustaining the prāṇa in the eyes. The deity (divine force) in the earth — it is that which relates to apāna in the person. The intermediate space (antarikṣa) is samāna."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara elaborates the macro-micro correspondence first introduced in Praśna 1. The external sun is to external prāṇa what the internal prāṇa is to sight within the eye — each is a manifestation of the same principle at different scales. The earth's deity corresponds to apāna (the downward force that draws things earthward), and the intermediate space (antarikṣa) corresponds to samāna (the equalising, distributing middle principle). The same prāṇa that governs the body also governs the cosmos — they are one reality at two scales.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees this as establishing the Lord's dual immanence: externally through the cosmic sun and earth and sky; internally through the prāṇas of the individual body. The correspondences (sun-prāṇa, earth-apāna, sky-samāna) show that the Lord's governance is seamlessly integrated across the cosmic and individual scales. Worship of the external natural forces is therefore continuous with, not separate from, internal yogic cultivation — both are forms of honouring the same Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva uses this verse to establish that the cosmic deities (Sūrya, the Earth-goddess, the sky-deity) are directly connected to the prāṇas within the individual body, because all are expressions of Viṣṇu's single śakti working at different scales. This validates both external ritual worship (of sun and earth as divine) and internal prāṇa-cultivation (of the vital forces within), because both are directed toward the same source. The unity of the cosmic and individual is real, not metaphorical.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo finds this verse mapping the cosmic and individual planes onto each other with great precision. The sun as external prāṇa illuminating the eye's prāṇa reflects the Integral vision of the supramental light (cosmic) inspiring the individual mind's higher faculties. Earth's deity as apāna reflects the Material-Plane's gravitational, rooting function in the individual vital body. The sky (antarikṣa) as samāna reflects the vital-mental plane's harmonising function. These are not metaphors but real correspondences between different scales of the same universal process.

verses.sec: Praśna 3 — The Origin and Functions of Prāṇa
verses.dev: तेजो ह वा उदानस्तस्मात्तस्य शान्तस्य पुनर्भवः तेनाक्ष्णा यं पश्यन्ति विज्ञानमयः प्राणाः ॥
verses.iast: tejo ha vā udānastasmaattasya śāntasya punarbhavaḥ tenākṣṇā yaṁ paśyanti vijñānamayaḥ prāṇāḥ ||
verses.trans: "Light (tejas) is indeed udāna. Therefore when tejas is extinguished, the person is reborn through the prāṇas. With that eye by which they see, the prāṇas are made of consciousness (vijñānamayāḥ)."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara explains that udāna is fire (tejas) — the upward-moving, luminous vital force. When the fire of life goes out (at death), the prāṇas carrying consciousness within them (vijñānamayāḥ) go to the next birth. 'With that eye by which they see' refers to the inner light of consciousness — vijñāna — that is the true seer behind all sensory perception. This verse takes us from the physiology of prāṇa toward the consciousness-principle (vijñāna) that underlies and inhabits prāṇa, pointing toward Ātman as the ultimate vijñāna.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees the identification of udāna with tejas (light/fire) as affirming the Lord's luminous nature within the individual — the inner light that rises upward at death is the Lord's own light (prakāśa) radiating through the soul. 'Vijñānamayāḥ prāṇāḥ' — prāṇas made of consciousness — reflects the Viśiṣṭādvaita teaching that spirit (cit) pervades all prāṇa, making the vital forces ultimately cognitive, not merely mechanical. The departure of vijñānamaya prāṇas in rebirth is the Lord directing the soul's journey through the compassionate law of karma.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva takes vijñānamayāḥ prāṇāḥ to mean that the prāṇas are permeated by the jīva's consciousness (vijñāna) — a point crucial for establishing the jīva's real distinctness from mere matter. The soul's prāṇas carry its vijñāna (individual consciousness) from life to life, maintaining the continuity of karma and identity across deaths. This is Viṣṇu's mechanism of karmic continuity: the vijñānamaya prāṇas are the vehicle through which the soul's accumulated merits and demerits are transported across lives under Viṣṇu's exact governance.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads vijñānamayāḥ prāṇāḥ as pointing to the consciousness-force nature of the Life-power — prāṇa is not mere physical breath but a force already infused with intelligence. This is the Integral vision: Life is a form of consciousness-energy (cit-śakti), not mere mechanical process. Udāna as tejas (luminous fire) is the aspiring consciousness that rises at death — the soul's luminous vital force that carries its evolutionary attainments forward into the next embodiment. Death is thus not loss but transformation and continuation of the evolutionary journey.

verses.sec: Praśna 3 — The Origin and Functions of Prāṇa
verses.dev: आकाशो ह वा एष प्राणो यद्वायुः स व्यानः अथ यदेतदसौ तपति स एव प्राणो यदापः स अपानः ॥
verses.iast: ākāśo ha vā eṣa prāṇo yadvāyuḥ sa vyānaḥ atha yadetadasau tapati sa eva prāṇo yadāpaḥ sa apānaḥ ||
verses.trans: "Space (ākāśa) is indeed prāṇa; air (vāyu) is vyāna (the diffusing breath); the sun that blazes is prāṇa itself; rain-water (āpaḥ) is apāna."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara completes the cosmic-individual correspondence: ākāśa (space) is prāṇa because both are all-pervasive; vāyu (air in all directions) is vyāna because both diffuse and pervade; the sun (blazing heat) is prāṇa proper because both are sources of vital energy; and rain-water (which falls downward) is apāna because both move downward. The five prāṇas exactly correspond to the five elemental forces, establishing that the bodily prāṇas and the cosmic forces are one and the same principle manifesting at different scales.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja uses these correspondences to support his vision of the body-cosmos homology: what governs the body governs the cosmos, because both are the Lord's body animated by the same divine prāṇa-śakti. The Lord who is the antaryāmin within the body is also the sovereign of the cosmos — one divine governance from within and without simultaneously. These identifications are not merely pedagogical but express the real unity of the Lord's self-expression across all scales of existence.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva accepts these cosmic-vital correspondences as real ontological connections, not mere analogies. Space, air, sun, and rain are not identical to ākāśa, vyāna, prāṇa, and apāna — they are the same Viṣṇu-energies operating at different scales. The cosmos and the body are both real and both governed by the same divine principles, because Viṣṇu has designed them to be structurally corresponding expressions of His own creative will. This is not pantheism but ordered analogical governance by a single sovereign.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees the four correspondences (space-prāṇa, air-vyāna, sun-prāṇa, water-apāna) as establishing the Integral truth that every natural force has both a cosmic and a microcosmic dimension — they are the same Force at different levels of its descent into Matter. In the Integral Yoga, working with the vital-prāṇa within necessarily affects one's relationship to the cosmic forces (air, fire, water, space) — and vice versa. The practitioner who opens to the cosmic prāṇa expands the individual vital being toward its universal source.

verses.sec: Praśna 3 — The Origin and Functions of Prāṇa
verses.dev: पृथिव्यपाने चक्षुरादित्ये तेजः श्रोत्रे दिशः प्रतिष्ठापितं एवमाकाशे दिशो विश्रमन्ते ॥
verses.iast: pṛthivyapāne cakṣurāditye tejaḥ śrotre diśaḥ pratiṣṭhāpitaṁ evamākāśe diśo viśramante ||
verses.trans: "Earth is established in apāna; the sun's light is established in the sight (cakṣus); the quarters (directions) are established in the hearing (śrotra). In this way (all the forces) come to rest in ākāśa."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara uses this verse to show the convergence of all cosmic forces into the prāṇas of the individual body — earth in apāna, solar light in sight, the quarters in hearing. All is ultimately established in ākāśa (space), which is itself the subtlest element, the closest to Brahman among the elements. This convergence in ākāśa anticipates the Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad's teaching that all states of consciousness ultimately 'rest' in the turīya (the fourth, transcendent state) — ākāśa as the space of pure consciousness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads the convergence of all forces in ākāśa as pointing to the Lord who is the space-like (ākāśavat) ground of all existence — infinite, pervasive, pure. Earth settling in apāna, light in sight, and directions in hearing all reflect the Lord's inner organisation of the body's relationship with the cosmos. Everything ultimately rests (viśramate) in ākāśa — the Lord's pervasive, pure nature that is the ground of all, the ultimate space of divine rest.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva takes the final settlement in ākāśa as pointing to Viṣṇu, whose nature is often described as ākāśa-like (infinite, pure, all-pervasive). The individual body's forces (sight, hearing, apāna) resting in ākāśa reflects how all cosmic and individual functions ultimately rest in and depend upon Viṣṇu. 'Viśramante' (come to rest) carries an eschatological overtone in Madhva's reading: the final rest of all forces in Viṣṇu is the symbol of mokṣa, the soul's final rest in Vaikuṇṭha.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees ākāśa as the causal plane — the infinite, unmanifest ground from which all cosmic forces emerge and into which they ultimately return. The convergence of earth in apāna, light in sight, and directions in hearing shows how the individual prāṇic body is a focused point of the cosmic energies. 'Viśramante' (come to rest) points to the state of integral repose where all the diverse cosmic energies are gathered into the luminous calm of the Supermind — the 'rest' that is not inertia but the peace of perfect divine organisation.

verses.sec: Praśna 3 — The Origin and Functions of Prāṇa
verses.dev: स एवं वेदात्मानं प्राणं प्रविश्य ईश्वरः सर्वस्यात्मा सर्वस्य ज्ञाता भवति ॥
verses.iast: sa evaṁ vedātmānaṁ prāṇaṁ praviśya īśvaraḥ sarvasya ātmā sarvasya jñātā bhavati ||
verses.trans: "He who thus knows prāṇa in this way, entering the Self, becomes the Lord (Īśvara), the Self of all, the knower of all."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara sees this verse as the climax of Praśna 3: the one who knows prāṇa in all its cosmic and individual dimensions, through that knowledge, enters the Ātman. In Advaita, 'entering the Self' (ātmānaṁ praviśya) is not a journey but a recognition — the realisation that one has always been the Ātman. 'The Lord, the Self of all, the knower of all' is the description of Brahman in its omniscient aspect. The seeker who realises Brahman does not become a separate Īśvara but recognises their identity with the one Brahman that always was Īśvara.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads 'becomes Īśvara, the Self of all, the knower of all' in the context of the liberated soul's experience. The soul, through bhakti and knowledge, enters so deeply into the Lord that it participates in His omniscience and universal selfhood — not becoming identical with God (which would collapse the eternal individual soul-God distinction) but being granted the experience of God's own omniscience as a gift of divine grace. This is the Viśiṣṭādvaita mokṣa: the soul's participation in divine omniscience and universal love, not merger.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva carefully reads 'becomes Īśvara, Self of all, knower of all' as applying to the jīva's liberated realisation rather than as a statement of ontological identity. The liberated soul, in Vaikuṇṭha, knows Viṣṇu (the true Īśvara, the true Self of all, the true knower of all) directly and fully — it does not become Viṣṇu. The verse must be interpreted in light of Madhva's strict difference between jīva and Īśvara: the jīva achieves the maximum possible closeness to Viṣṇu without ever ceasing to be a distinct, dependent soul.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads this verse as describing the supramental realisation: the seeker who has understood the Life-force in all its cosmic and individual dimensions, and followed it back to the Ātman, awakens as the conscious Īśvara — the Self of all, the knower of all. This is not a personal achievement but the recognition of what was always true: the consciousness that knows the Life-force in all its forms is the same consciousness that is the Life-force itself. In Aurobindo's Integral framework, this realisation is the basis for the complete transformation of life — not an escape but a new quality of universal creative engagement.

verses.sec: Praśna 4 — Sleep, Dream, and Deep Sleep
verses.dev: अथ हैनं सौर्यायणी गार्ग्यः पप्रच्छ भगवन् को न्वस्मिन् पुरुषे शेते को जागर्ति कतरो ह्येष देवः स्वप्नान्पश्यति कस्यैतत्सुखं भवति कस्मिन्नु सर्वे संप्रतिष्ठिता भवन्तीति ॥
verses.iast: atha hainaṁ sauryāyaṇī gārgyaḥ papraccha bhagavan ko nvасmin puruṣe śete ko jāgarti kataro hyeṣа devaḥ svapnānpaśyati kasyaitatsukhaṁ bhavati kasminnusarve saṁpratiṣṭhitā bhavantīti ||
verses.trans: Then Sauryāyaṇī Gārgya asked: "Venerable sir, who sleeps in this person? Who is awake? Which of these is the deva (divine faculty) who sees dreams? Whose is the happiness (that arises in deep sleep)? On what do all of them rest?"

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara considers this the most philosophically significant question of the Upaniṣad, touching directly on the nature of consciousness across its three states: waking (jāgrat), dream (svapna), and deep sleep (suṣupti). The question 'who sleeps? who is awake?' is not physiological but metaphysical: what is the subject of these changing states? Advaita's answer will be that the unchanging Ātman is always awake — it is merely that the psychophysical complex (mind-body) that appears to be 'the person' sleeps. The turīya (fourth) as the substratum of all three states is implicit in the question 'on what do all rest?'
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja finds in these five questions the key to understanding the soul's relationship with the Lord across all states of consciousness. The sleeping person, the dreaming person, the person in blissful deep sleep — in each state, the Lord (as antaryāmin, inner ruler) is present and sustaining the soul. The question 'on what do all rest?' points to the Lord as the universal ground — the soul rests in the Lord in every state, most vividly in deep sleep where it is most directly in the Lord's embrace without the mediation of the senses and mind.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads these questions as a complete inquiry into the soul's condition across the three states and its relationship to Viṣṇu in each. In waking, the soul is active; in dream, it experiences karmic projections; in deep sleep, it merges into a kind of Viṣṇu-sustained rest. But in none of these states does the soul become Viṣṇu or cease to be distinct from Him. 'On what do all rest?' has a definitive Dvaita answer: all rest on Viṣṇu, who remains the eternal, unchanging support of the jīva across all its experiential states.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees these five questions as mapping the full psychic geography that the Integral Yoga must navigate. Waking (jāgrat) is the surface consciousness; dream (svapna) reveals the inner subtle-vital and mental worlds; deep sleep (suṣupti) is the soul's immersion in the causal depths. The question 'on what do all rest?' points to the subliminal and supramental ground that underlies all three. Aurobindo's own exploration of the inner worlds of dream and sleep formed an important part of his integral research, and this question is the Upaniṣadic sanction for that inquiry.

verses.sec: Praśna 4 — Sleep, Dream, and Deep Sleep
verses.dev: तस्मै स होवाच यथा हि गार्ग्य मरीचयोऽर्कस्यास्तं गच्छतः सर्वा एतस्मिंस्तेजोमण्डले एकीभवन्ति ताः पुनः पुनरुदयतः प्रचरन्त्येवमह सर्वे देवाः मनसि एकीभवन्ति ॥
verses.iast: tasmai sa hovāca yathā hi gārgya marīcayo'rkasyāstaṁ gacchatah sarvā etasmiṁstejomaṇḍale ekībhavanti tāḥ punaḥ punarudayataḥ pracarantyevamaha sarve devāḥ manasi ekībhavanti ||
verses.trans: He said to him: "Gārgya, just as all the rays of the setting sun are gathered into that solar orb of light, and when it rises again they pour forth again — in the same way, all the devas (senses and faculties) are gathered into the mind (manas) (when one falls asleep)."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara finds the sun-rays analogy perfect for describing the nightly convergence of the sense organs into the mind in sleep. The rays do not cease to exist when gathered into the sun — they are there in potential, ready to pour forth again. Similarly, the senses do not die in sleep but are absorbed into their subtle basis, the mind. This convergence reveals the mind as more fundamental than the individual senses — and the question 'what is more fundamental than the mind?' will be answered through the praśna's teaching on the Self that underlies even the sleeping mind.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja appreciates this image for showing that even in sleep, the Lord continues to hold all the soul's faculties in unity within the mind. The gathering of the rays into the sun's orb is the Lord drawing the soul's scattered energies into a temporary unity — a foretaste of the final unity of the liberated soul resting in the Lord. The sunrise-reawakening of the rays corresponds to the soul's return to waking life under the Lord's direction, each day a small cycle of the great cosmic rhythm of involution and evolution.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva uses this analogy to establish that the individual senses and their substrate mind are always dependent on a higher organizing principle — just as rays depend on the sun. In sleep, Viṣṇu draws the senses into the mind under His governance; in waking, He releases them. The soul has no independent power over its own waking and sleeping — this rhythm is entirely under Viṣṇu's control. This enforces the Dvaita insight that even the most intimate biological rhythms of the soul's life are expressions of divine governance.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads the solar analogy as pointing to the subliminal Self (the 'sun') and the surface faculties (the 'rays'). In sleep, the surface consciousness withdraws into the subliminal — a natural return to the deeper source. For the ordinary person this produces unconscious sleep; for the yogi it can be conscious exploration of the inner worlds. The daily rhythm of waking-sleep is the microcosmic version of the cosmic cycle of manifestation-withdrawal. Understanding this rhythm is the beginning of the inner psychology that integral yoga requires.

verses.sec: Praśna 4 — Sleep, Dream, and Deep Sleep
verses.dev: तत्र यदिदं मनः संप्रसुप्तं भवति सोऽयमग्नौ वागप्येति प्राणो वाचि स इत्वा श्रोत्रस्य श्रोत्रमनसः ॥
verses.iast: tatra yadidaṁ manaḥ saṁprasuptaṁ bhavati so'yamagnau vāgapyeti prāṇo vāci sa itvā śrotrasya śrotramanas ||
verses.trans: "There, when the mind is fully absorbed in deep sleep, speech (vāk) merges into fire (agni), the breath (prāṇa) merges into speech, hearing merges into the ear (space), and the ear into the mind."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara traces the cascade of absorption in deep sleep: speech → fire, prāṇa → speech (or the organ of speech), hearing → space, and sight → the sun. This cascade reveals the hierarchy of subtle behind gross — each sense dissolves into its cosmic counterpart at the causal level when the mind is in suṣupti. The final absorption of all into the mind points toward the Ātman that underlies even the mind — the teaching will build toward the recognition that the mind itself dissolves in Ātman in the deepest state.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees the cascade of absorption as the Lord drawing each faculty back to its elemental source — a nightly pralaya (dissolution) in miniature. Speech returning to fire, prāṇa to speech, hearing to space — each follows the reverse order of the cosmic creation (fire produces speech, speech produces prāṇa, etc.). This reverse-creation in sleep reflects the Lord's sovereign power over the order of manifestation. In the morning, He reverses the reversal, re-creating the individual's waking experience.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes the precise ladder of absorption: each faculty returns to its cosmic counterpart at night under Viṣṇu's direction. This nightly ritual of dissolution is not accidental but is Viṣṇu's governance of the soul's biological rhythm. The fact that these faculties can be precisely accounted for (speech → fire, etc.) demonstrates the meticulous architecture of Viṣṇu's cosmos — nothing is vague or chaotic. The soul's rest in sleep is a gift of Viṣṇu's grace, restoring it for another day of karmic working-out.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads this cascade as mapping the nightly return of the surface consciousness to deeper and deeper levels. Speech dissolving into fire (the vital-physical level), prāṇa returning to its speech-origin (the vital level), hearing returning to cosmic space (the mental-causal level) — this is the soul's nightly descent through its own sheaths toward the causal ground. In the Integral Yoga, the developed practitioner can maintain awareness through these successive levels of withdrawal, using sleep as a genuine spiritual practice of inner exploration.

verses.sec: Praśna 4 — Sleep, Dream, and Deep Sleep
verses.dev: तत् तदा स देवः स्वप्ने महिमानमनुभवति यद्दृष्टं दृष्टमनुपश्यति श्रुतं श्रुतमेवार्थमनुशृणोति देशान्दिगन्तर्विषयान् च प्रत्यनुभवत्यदृष्टं च दृष्टं च श्रुतं चाश्रुतं च अनुभूतं चानुभूतं च सर्वं पश्यति सर्वः पश्यति ॥
verses.iast: tat tadā sa devaḥ svapne mahimānamanubhavati yaddṛṣṭaṁ dṛṣṭamanupasyati śrutaṁ śrutamevārthamanusṛṇoti deśāndidantarviṣayāṁśca pratyanubhavatyAdṛṣṭaṁ ca dṛṣṭaṁ ca śrutaṁ cāśrutaṁ ca anubhūtaṁ cānubhūtaṁ ca sarvaṁ pasyati sarvaḥ pasyati ||
verses.trans: "Then in that state, the deva (the mind as dreamer) experiences its own greatness. It sees again what was seen, hears again what was heard, experiences again countries and directions and other regions. It sees what was seen and unseen, heard and unheard, experienced and unexperienced — all of it. It sees everything; it becomes everything."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara notes that the mind in the dream state (svapna) creates its own world from the impressions (saṃskāras) stored in it — it is both the creator and the experiencer of the dream world. 'It sees everything; it becomes everything' (sarvaḥ paśyati) is a remarkable statement of the dream-mind's creative omnipotence within its own projected world. For Advaita, this points toward the same creative omnipotence of Brahman in relation to the waking cosmos — both are projections, though the waking projection is shared and the dream projection is individual.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees the dream experience as the Lord granting the soul a vast inner experience that transcends ordinary waking limitations. The soul in dream can revisit the seen, experience the unseen, travel beyond physical limits — because the Lord, from within, opens inner domains of experience appropriate to the soul's karmic and devotional state. The 'greatness' (mahiman) the dreamer experiences is a taste of the soul's essential freedom from bodily limits, a foretaste of the soul's liberation when it will dwell in the infinite world of the Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads dream experience as Viṣṇu granting the jīva a field of karmic experience without the mediation of the external world. The dream world is real to the dreamer, not illusory — Viṣṇu creates it from the soul's accumulated saṃskāras for the purpose of karmic processing. The fact that the dreamer 'sees what was seen and unseen' indicates that the dream field is not merely memory but a Viṣṇu-created theatre of fuller karmic experience than waking alone could provide.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo finds the dream state crucial for the Integral Yoga's understanding of the inner worlds. The dreaming mind's access to seen and unseen, heard and unheard, experienced and unexperienced realities reflects the fact that the dream-state provides access to the subliminal consciousness — the vast inner world that contains much more than the surface mind ordinarily knows. Aurobindo spent considerable attention on the psychic significance of the inner planes accessed in dream: they are not illusions but real dimensions of consciousness, more plastic and direct than the physical world.

verses.sec: Praśna 4 — Sleep, Dream, and Deep Sleep
verses.dev: यदा तु सुप्तस्य न कञ्चन स्वप्नं पश्यति अथास्मिन् प्राणे एवैकीभवति तदास्य वाक्सर्वे प्राणे एकीभवन्ति ॥
verses.iast: yadā tu suptasya na kañcana svapnaṁ paśyati athāsmin prāṇe evaikikbhavati tadāsya vāksarve prāṇe ekībhavanti ||
verses.trans: "But when the sleeper sees no dream at all, then he becomes one with prāṇa alone. Then his speech and all the prāṇas are absorbed in prāṇa (the root vital)."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara explains this as the transition from svapna (dream) to suṣupti (deep dreamless sleep). When even the dream-mind is stilled, the individual prāṇas converge into the root prāṇa, which itself is then absorbed into the Ātman. This state — dreamless, silent, without any object of consciousness — is the closest the ordinary person comes to the experience of Brahman in daily life. Śaṅkara notes the happiness of deep sleep as evidence of the Ātman's blissful nature — for the source of the happiness in suṣupti is the proximity to Ātman, not the presence of any external object.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads suṣupti as the soul's most intimate resting place in the Lord's embrace during embodied life. When all functions converge into prāṇa and prāṇa into the Ātman-Lord, the soul rests in the Lord's heart without the mediation of senses, mind, or even dreams. The bliss experienced in this state is the bliss of the Lord's own ānanda radiating to the resting soul. Rāmānuja sees this as pointing toward liberation: mokṣa is a permanent suṣupti in the Lord's presence — but conscious, not unconscious as in ordinary sleep.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva describes deep sleep as the jīva's absorption into a condition of minimal Viṣṇu-contact — the soul is held in Viṣṇu's sustaining grip without the intermediary of mind or senses. The convergence of all prāṇas into root prāṇa and then into the Ātman-ground (Viṣṇu's śakti within the body) is a temporary dissolution that prefigures, but is not identical to, liberation. In liberation, the soul consciously rests in Viṣṇu with full awareness; in suṣupti, it rests there without self-awareness, the ignorance remaining intact.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees suṣupti (dreamless sleep) as the soul's immersion in the causal consciousness — the unmanifest ground from which all mental and vital activity springs. In this state, the surface being is at rest and only the deep soul (psychic being) and the causal body continue. The absorption of all prāṇas into the root prāṇa mirrors the great pralaya at the cosmic level. In the Integral framework, the practitioner of deep meditation can access this state consciously, using it to touch the causal ground of being and draw transformative energy from it.

verses.sec: Praśna 4 — Sleep, Dream, and Deep Sleep
verses.dev: तद्यथा स्वप्ने संप्रसाद आकाशे भवति स एष आत्मोऽभयं हि वा एतदिति एतत् ब्रह्म देवाः सर्व एतस्मिन् संप्रतिष्ठिता भवन्ति ॥
verses.iast: tadyathā svapne saṁprasāda ākāśe bhavati sa eṣa ātmo'bhayaṁ hi vā etaditi etat brahma devāḥ sarva etasmin saṁpratiṣṭhitā bhavanti ||
verses.trans: "Just as a bird (in flight) comes to rest on a tree — in the same way, in deep sleep, this (person) comes to rest in the Ātman. In that (deep sleep), all devas (faculties and senses) find their rest. The Ātman is the fearless; it is the immortal Brahman. In it all the devas are established."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara sees this as the most direct Advaita statement in the Praśna Upaniṣad: in deep sleep, the person rests in the Ātman, which is the fearless (abhaya), the immortal Brahman. 'All the devas are established in it' means all faculties find their ultimate ground in Brahman. The bird-on-tree analogy is perfect: the bird (the waking/dreaming ego) is only temporarily at rest in the tree (Ātman) during sleep; the sage who has known the Ātman makes this rest permanent, no longer taking flight into the samsāric dream of waking existence.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads 'the Ātman that is the fearless Brahman' as the Lord Himself — the supreme Self in whom all the soul's faculties find their rest. The bird coming to its tree is an image of the soul finding its home in the Lord. 'Fearless' (abhaya) is a key divine attribute in Viśiṣṭādvaita: the Lord is the one who grants abhaya (fearlessness) to the surrendered soul — a formal theological principle. All devas being established in Brahman shows that the Lord is the ground of all cosmic functioning, the tree that supports all the 'birds' of divine powers.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads this as the soul momentarily finding rest in Viṣṇu during deep sleep — not union but proximity. The 'fearless, immortal Brahman' in whom all devas are established is Viṣṇu Himself. The soul's nightly rest in Viṣṇu is a temporary grace, a preview of the eternal rest of liberation. 'All devas established in it' affirms Viṣṇu's supreme sovereignty as the ground of all divine functions. The bird-tree image in Dvaita is bittersweet: the bird of the soul currently only lands temporarily on the tree of Viṣṇu — liberation means it never needs to fly away again.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads the bird-on-tree as the soul finding its deepest rest in the causal Ātman during suṣupti. 'Fearless, immortal Brahman' is the description of the supramental ground — infinite, deathless, without any fear because fear requires the perception of an 'other.' In the Integral framework, the deep sleep state gives the ordinary person a nightly taste of the Supramental Peace (the śānti that passes understanding). The practitioner of integral yoga cultivates this peace consciously as the basis for all work — 'all devas established in it' means all cosmic creative energies operate from this ground of peace.

verses.sec: Praśna 4 — Sleep, Dream, and Deep Sleep
verses.dev: यथा सोम्यैते नद्यः स्यन्दमानाः समुद्राभिगच्छन्ति तासां नामरूपे प्रजहाति स्यन्दमानानां पुनरागमने तदेवं देव गर्हापत्यो ह्यग्निः ॥
verses.iast: yathā somyaite nadyaḥ syandamānāḥ samudrābhigacchanti tāsāṁ nāmarūpe prajahāti syandamānānāṁ punarāgamane tadevam deva gārhāpatyo hyagniḥ ||
verses.trans: "Dear one, just as these rivers flowing to the sea abandon their name and form — and when they return they assume their name and form again — in the same way, the gārhapatya fire is this deva (prāṇa)."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara uses the rivers-into-sea analogy — one of the most famous in Vedānta — to teach that individuality dissolves in the Ātman just as rivers lose their identity in the ocean. In suṣupti, the individual faculties lose their names and forms and merge into the prāṇa-ground/Ātman. Their daily 'return' is the re-emergence of individuality from the non-dual ground each morning. For Advaita, this shows that individuality is provisional — the ocean (Brahman) alone is real; the rivers (individual selves) are Brahman temporarily taking the form of rivers.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads the rivers-into-sea analogy carefully: even in the sea, the river-waters remain distinct constituents — they do not literally become the sea's substance. Similarly, the individual souls and their faculties that 'merge' into Brahman in deep sleep retain their essential distinctness even while sharing in Brahman's unity. This is the Viśiṣṭādvaita interpretation of apparent merger: real unity-in-distinction, not the Advaita collapse into non-difference. The gārhapatya fire as prāṇa connects this to the ritual fire-tradition.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva firmly resists the apparent implication of merger in the river-sea image. Rivers entering the sea do not literally become the sea — they retain their existence as water even if their individual names temporarily disappear. So the jīva in deep sleep or at dissolution retains its essential nature even when its functions are absorbed. The gārhapatya fire as deva-prāṇa shows that ritual fire and vital breath are both expressions of the same divine fire-principle that Viṣṇu has established in the cosmos and the body.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo finds the rivers-into-sea image one of the great symbols of the Upaniṣads — the individual consciousnesses flowing into the universal. But in the Integral reading, the rivers do not lose their identity permanently; they return with the rains to flow again, enriched by their time in the sea. This cycle — differentiation, integration, re-differentiation — is the rhythm of consciousness evolving. The point is not to remain permanently merged in the sea but to carry the sea's depth and fullness back into the rivers of individual existence. This is the Integral alternative to mere mokṣa.

verses.sec: Praśna 4 — Sleep, Dream, and Deep Sleep
verses.dev: मनो ह वा एष देवो दिवसे मनसा रात्रिं चरति सुप्तो नोत्तिष्ठति स हि सर्वान् कामान् पश्यति ॥
verses.iast: mano ha vā eṣa devo divase manasā rātriṁ carati supto nottṣṭhati sa hi sarvān kāmān paśyati ||
verses.trans: "The mind alone is this deva (divine faculty). During the day it wanders as mind; in the night it wanders too (in dream). It does not set (like the sun) — it sees all desires (experiences)."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara highlights the mind's unique status as the 'deva that never sets': unlike the senses (sight, hearing) which cease to function in deep sleep, the mind-principle continues even in suṣupti in its subtlest form. 'It sees all desires' refers to the dream state where the mind projects its own desires as experiences. This prepares the way for the final teaching: what remains when even this ceaselessly active mind is stilled? The Ātman — the eternal witness that is behind even the dreaming mind.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja notes that the mind's ceaseless activity across day and night — waking and dream — reflects the soul's inherent restlessness outside of the Lord. The mind that 'never sets' is simultaneously the source of dream experience and the obstacle to the rest of suṣupti. True rest comes only when the mind, through bhakti, is fully surrendered to the Lord — and the 'mind that sees all desires' becomes instead the mind that sees only the Lord. This transformation of desire (kāma) into devotion (bhakti) is the Viśiṣṭādvaita path.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva understands 'the mind sees all desires' as Viṣṇu presenting the soul's accumulated desires (kāmas) to it in the dream state for the purpose of gradual purification. The mind's ceaseless activity is not a defect but the mechanism through which Viṣṇu processes the soul's karma across the three states of consciousness. The 'mind that never sets' is a description of the jīva's perpetual activity under divine governance — it is always awake to its karmic obligations, even in sleep.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads 'the mind that never sets' as pointing to the subliminal mental consciousness that continues below the surface even in sleep. This is the inner mental being that Aurobindo describes as more vast and continuous than the surface mind — it has access to experiences across all states and times. 'Seeing all desires' is the mind accessing the full range of subliminal content in dream. The Integral Yoga's work with the inner mind — learning to observe, purify, and redirect its desires — begins with recognising this never-setting quality of the mental consciousness.

verses.sec: Praśna 4 — Sleep, Dream, and Deep Sleep
verses.dev: यत्र तद्रात्र्यां संप्रसुप्तः स्वप्ने दृष्ट्वा पुण्यं पापं च अनेकाश्च प्राणाः तत् सर्वं शेते सर्वः सुप्तः ॥
verses.iast: yatra tadrātryāṁ saṁprasuptaḥ svapne dṛṣṭvā puṇyaṁ pāpaṁ ca anekāśca prāṇāḥ tat sarvaṁ śete sarvaḥ suptaḥ ||
verses.trans: "Where, in the night, the sleeping one has seen virtue (puṇya) and sin (pāpa) in dream, and many other prāṇas (experiences) — all that sleeps; the whole person sleeps."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara explains that in the deepest phase of sleep (suṣupti), even the dream experiences of virtue and sin are absorbed — nothing remains active. 'The whole person sleeps' (sarvaḥ suptaḥ) means the complete psychophysical complex, including the dream-mind with its moral experiences, is fully at rest. This total rest points toward the Ātman as the only thing 'awake' in this state — the unchanging witness of even the sleeping state. The bliss of this total rest is the bliss of the Ātman, which ordinary experience has in its shadow.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads the sleep of puṇya and pāpa (virtue and sin) as the suspension of the soul's karmic engagement — in deep sleep, karma temporarily stops accruing or being experienced. The soul rests in the Lord beyond both merit and demerit. This momentary transcendence of the good-evil duality in deep sleep foreshadows the liberated state where the soul, dwelling in the Lord, is beyond karmic engagement altogether. The Lord's embrace in suṣupti is thus a merciful preview of the final liberation from the moral-karmic cycle.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that even the experiences of virtue and sin encountered in dream are absorbed in suṣupti, showing that Viṣṇu's governance in deep sleep takes the soul beyond even its karmic experiences. This does not mean karma is suspended — it continues to accumulate in subtler forms. But the experiential intensity of the waking and dream states is temporarily dissolved, giving the soul rest. 'The whole person sleeps' means Viṣṇu has temporarily withdrawn all the soul's active engagement with its karmic field, granting it restoration.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads 'virtue and sin both sleep' as the suspension of the moral ego in the deepest level of sleep — the soul enters a state beyond the ethical dualities that govern waking and dream. This is significant in the Integral framework: the supramental consciousness that Aurobindo sought to bring down is itself 'beyond good and evil' in the sense that it operates from a level of truth where the dualities of the moral mind no longer apply. Suṣupti is the ordinary person's nightly taste of this post-moral condition of consciousness.

verses.sec: Praśna 4 — Sleep, Dream, and Deep Sleep
verses.dev: यथाग्नौ समिधः समग्रा अस्तं यन्ति अथ पुनः प्रज्वलन्ति एवमेव हैतस्मादात्मनः सर्वे प्राणाः प्रतिप्रसवन्ति ॥
verses.iast: yathāgnau samidhaḥ samagrā astaṁ yanti atha punaḥ prajvalanti evameva haitasmādātmanaḥ sarve prāṇāḥ pratiprasavanti ||
verses.trans: "Just as firewood (samidhas) is completely consumed into fire and then blazes again — in the same way, from this Ātman, all the prāṇas re-emerge."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara uses the fire-and-firewood image to teach the cycle of cosmic involvement and return. The prāṇas 'consumed' into the Ātman in deep sleep re-emerge (pratiprasavanti) as the morning of waking. For Advaita, the firewood image is slightly imperfect — firewood is transformed by fire, whereas the prāṇas re-emerge from the Ātman unchanged (except by their karmic momentum). The deeper point: the Ātman is the source and ground from which all prāṇas repeatedly emerge and into which they repeatedly dissolve — this is the ceaseless cycle of saṃsāra until liberation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja finds the re-emergence of all prāṇas from the Ātman (the Lord) each morning to be an image of the Lord's perpetual creative act. Just as the fire can re-ignite the firewood, the Lord re-manifests the soul's faculties each day — this is the Lord's creative grace continuously operating. The cycle of absorption-and-re-emergence is the Lord's own rhythm of involution and evolution at the individual scale. Knowing this, the devotee greets each morning as a gift of divine re-creation — a new manifestation of the Lord's grace.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads the re-emergence of prāṇas from Ātman (Viṣṇu) each morning as Viṣṇu's continual act of granting life to the jīva. The soul is entirely dependent on Viṣṇu's will for its daily re-awakening — nothing happens automatically. The fire-firewood analogy shows a real transformation at the material level (wood to ash to heat) which is not perfect for souls (which remain unchanged in essence), but the image captures the essential point: Viṣṇu is the inexhaustible fire from which the soul's daily life repeatedly blazes forth.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo finds the fire-and-wood image of re-emergence one of the most powerful in the Upaniṣad. Each morning, the prāṇas re-emerge from the causal Ātman enriched by their nightly immersion — or so they could be, for the practitioner who maintains awareness. In the Integral Yoga, the morning re-emergence from sleep can become a conscious act of re-dedication — the prāṇas re-igniting in the fire of the divine Ātman, blazing with renewed aspiration. This transforms the mundane rhythm of waking-sleep into a daily cycle of spiritual renewal.

verses.sec: Praśna 4 — Sleep, Dream, and Deep Sleep
verses.dev: प्राणाग्नय एव स्मिन् पुरे जाग्रति प्राणान् देव आत्मा निदधे आत्मनि विद्वान्न कञ्चन शोचति ॥
verses.iast: prāṇāgnaya eva smin pure jāgrati prāṇān deva ātmā nidadhе ātmani vidvānna kañcana śocati ||
verses.trans: "The prāṇa-fires alone remain awake in this city (of the body). The divine Self (deva ātmā) places the prāṇas in the Ātman. The wise one who knows this does not grieve for anything."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara highlights two teachings: first, that the prāṇa-fires (the five vital functions) remain active even in deep sleep — the body remains alive because prāṇa continues. Second, and most importantly, 'the divine Self places the prāṇas in the Ātman' — the ultimate ground is the Ātman, not prāṇa. The 'wise one who knows this does not grieve' is the classic Upaniṣadic formula for liberation: the knower of Brahman transcends grief because grief arises from the illusion of separation from the ultimate. Knowing the Ātman as one's own nature, there is nothing to fear and nothing to grieve.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads 'the divine Self places the prāṇas in the Ātman' as the Lord drawing all the soul's vital functions into His own heart in deep sleep — an act of intimate divine sustenance. 'The wise one who does not grieve' is the devotee who, knowing that all is held by the Lord's grace across all states of consciousness, rests in complete surrender. Grief arises from the sense that one is unprotected, that beloved things can be lost — but the devotee who knows the Lord's perpetual sustaining presence has nothing to fear.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva sees 'the deva ātmā' who places prāṇas in the Ātman as Viṣṇu Himself, the divine Self who places all the soul's vital functions within His own protective awareness during sleep. 'The wise one who does not grieve' is the devotee who knows Viṣṇu's complete sovereignty and protection — having understood that Viṣṇu governs every state of the soul's existence (waking, dream, sleep), there is no possible state in which one is outside Viṣṇu's care. This knowledge destroys grief at its root, leaving the devotee in the permanent peace of surrender.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads 'the divine Self placing prāṇas in the Ātman' as the supramental consciousness gathering the vital energies into the divine ground — the natural state of the fully transformed being. 'The wise one who knows this does not grieve' is the description of the integral realiser — not one who has escaped from life but one who has discovered the deathless ground beneath all life's changes. The prāṇa-fires remaining awake in the body-city reflect the continued engagement with life; their placement in the Ātman reflects the inner freedom. This is the integral balance: full life + full knowledge.

verses.dev: अथ हैनं शैब्यः सत्यकामः पप्रच्छ स यो ह वा एतद्भगवन् मनुष्येषु प्रायणान्तमोङ्कारमभिध्यायीत कतमं वाव स तेन लोकं जयतीति ॥
verses.iast: atha hainaṁ śaibyaḥ satyakāmaḥ papraccha sa yo ha vā etadbhagavan manuṣyeṣu prāyaṇāntamoṅkāramabhidhyāyīta katamaṁ vāva sa tena lokaṁ jayatīti ||
verses.trans: Then Śaibya Satyakāma asked: "Venerable sir, he among men who meditates on Om (Oṃkāra) until death — which world does he win by it?"

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara notes that this question about the fruit of Om-meditation at the moment of death brings together the two great Upaniṣadic themes: sound (śabda-brahma) and liberation. The question asks specifically about meditating on Om 'until death' (prāyaṇānta) — the supreme moment when the quality of one's final meditation determines the direction of the departing soul. Śaṅkara's answer will distinguish between meditating on Om as saguna Brahman (which leads to higher worlds) and as nirguna Brahman (which leads to liberation itself) — both being valid but leading to different levels of realisation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees Om as the most sacred syllable that directly points to the Lord Nārāyaṇa — who is both the lower Brahman (saguṇa, with form and qualities) and the higher Brahman (the transcendent ground). Meditating on Om 'until death' with love for the personal Lord leads to the highest liberation. The Nārāyaṇa tradition's emphasis on the praṇava (Om) as the name and form of Viṣṇu gives this question particular intensity: Śaibya is asking not about mere mantric technique but about the ultimate destiny of the devotee who loves the Lord supremely.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva identifies Om explicitly as the name of Viṣṇu — the praṇava that encodes the Lord's essential nature. Meditating on Om until death is the highest form of Viṣṇu-upāsanā at the moment of death, which Madhva considers the most spiritually significant moment of a human life. The world won by this meditation depends on the depth of understanding: those who know Om as both mātrās and supra-mātrā Brahman (Viṣṇu) attain the highest liberation. This question inaugurates the Praśna's most theologically dense teaching.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads Om as the vibrational ground of all manifestation — the primal sound from which the cosmic creation proceeds and to which it returns. Meditating on Om at death is the act of consciously returning one's consciousness to its source — the primal vibration of Existence-Consciousness-Bliss. In the Integral framework, the moment of death is the soul's greatest opportunity for supramental breakthrough: if consciousness can remain awake and centred in Om through the dissolution of the bodily instruments, it can achieve what ordinary meditation only approaches asymptotically.

verses.dev: एतद्वै सत्यकाम परं चापरं च ब्रह्म यदोङ्कारः तस्मादेतेन विद्वानेतेनैवायतनेनान्यतरमन्वेति ॥
verses.iast: etadvai satyakāma paraṁ cāparaṁ ca brahma yadoṅkāraḥ tasmādetena vidvānetenaivāyatanena anyataramanveti ||
verses.trans: "Satyakāma, Om is indeed both the higher (para) and the lower (apara) Brahman. Therefore the wise one, by means of this support (āyatana), attains one or the other."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara considers this the most comprehensive statement on Om in the entire Upaniṣadic corpus. Om is both para Brahman (the nirguna, transcendent, attributeless Absolute) and apara Brahman (the saguna, manifest, qualified Lord). This non-exclusive identification means that Om can serve as the gateway to either level of Brahman, depending on the meditator's depth of understanding. The 'support' (āyatana) language reflects that Om is a vibrational bridge — the sound-form of Brahman serves as the āyatana (resting place, vehicle, means) for the mind's journey toward the unspeakable Brahman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja agrees that Om encompasses both the higher and lower Brahman — but in Viśiṣṭādvaita, even the 'higher Brahman' is the personal Lord Nārāyaṇa in His transcendent aspect, not an impersonal absolute. Om as āyatana (support) is the devotee's most intimate connection with the Lord during practice — the sound of the Lord's own name resonating in the heart of the meditator. The 'wise one attains one or the other' reflects the gradient of bhakti-intensity: deeper devotion reaches the Lord's higher nature; shallower devotion reaches His lower (saguna) aspect.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva interprets 'higher and lower Brahman' as two aspects of Viṣṇu: His transcendent, fully independent nature (para) and His immanent, world-sustaining aspect (apara). Both are Viṣṇu. Om as the support for meditating on both aspects is the supreme yantra of Viṣṇu-upāsanā. The 'wise one' who meditates on Om with full knowledge of Viṣṇu's nature as both transcendent and immanent reaches the highest liberation; those who meditate on only one aspect reach intermediate worlds appropriate to that partial understanding.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo finds this one of the most important verses in all the Upaniṣads. Om as both para and apara Brahman captures the Integral truth: the divine is simultaneously the transcendent (para, the silent Infinite) and the immanent (apara, the dynamic creative Force). Neither the path of pure transcendence (seeking only the para) nor the path of pure immanence (seeking only the apara) is complete; the integral seeker, through Om, seeks the one Reality that is simultaneously both. This is the mantra-philosophy that underpins Aurobindo's own integral non-dualism.

verses.dev: स यद्येकमात्रमभिध्यायीत स तेनैव संविदितः सत्वरमेव जगत्याभिसंपद्यते तमृचो मनुष्यलोकमुपनयन्ते स तत्र तपसा ब्रह्मचर्येण श्रद्धया संपन्नो महिमानमनुभवति ॥
verses.iast: sa yadyekamātramabhidhyāyīta sa tenaiva saṁviditaḥ satvarameva jagatyābhisaṁpadyate tamṛco manuṣyalokamuplanayante sa tatra tapasā brahmacaryeṇa śraddhayā saṁpanno mahimānamanubhavati ||
verses.trans: "If he meditates on one mātrā (the sound 'A'), he is quickly awakened and returns to earth (the human world). The Ṛgvedic hymns lead him to the world of men. There, endowed with austerity, brahmacharya, and faith, he experiences greatness."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara explains that the three mātrās of Om (A, U, M) correspond to three levels of liberation. The first mātrā 'A' (the sound that opens the mouth, the basis of all speech) connects to the waking state, the Ṛgveda, and the world of men (manuṣyaloka). Meditating on Om as just 'A' leads to a virtuous rebirth in the human world — the fruit is excellent (greatness, tapas, brahmacharya) but not liberation, because the meditation does not penetrate to Om's full depth. This graduated teaching is characteristic of the Praśna Upaniṣad's pedagogical precision.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads the three-mātrā teaching as reflecting three levels of devotional depth. The single-mātrā meditator achieves an elevated human rebirth — a platform for deeper devotional development. The Ṛgvedic hymns that 'lead him' to the human world are the liturgical expressions of the Lord's saguna aspect at the most concrete level. This is not failure but preparation — the human world with its opportunities for tapas, brahmacharya, and śraddhā is the optimal field for deepening devotion toward the full realisation of Om.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva sees the graduated teaching on Om's mātrās as Pippalāda's precise instruction on the levels of Viṣṇu-upāsanā. Single-mātrā meditation, while genuine, yields only limited fruit: a good human rebirth. The connection to Ṛgveda hymns is significant — the Ṛgveda's karmic-ritual domain corresponds to the human world. This teaching reinforces Dvaita's insistence that knowledge and devotion must be complete to yield complete liberation: partial engagement with Om yields partial results, not because Viṣṇu is stingy but because the devotee has only partially opened.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads the first mātrā 'A' as corresponding to the physical-vital level of consciousness — the gross manifestation, the waking state. Meditation at this level leads back to the human world for further development — not failure but the natural trajectory of a consciousness still rooted in the physical-vital plane. The 'greatness' experienced through tapas, brahmacharya, and śraddhā in the human world is the greatness of a highly developed vital-physical being — the flower of evolutionary attainment at this level.

verses.dev: अथ यदि द्विमात्रेण मनसि संपद्यते सोऽन्तरिक्षं यजुर्भिरुन्नीयते सोमलोकं स सोमलोके विभूतिमनुभूय पुनरावर्तते ॥
verses.iast: atha yadi dvimātreṇa manasi saṁpadyate so'ntarikṣaṁ yajurbhirunnīyate somalokaṁ sa somaloке vibhūtimanubhūya punarāvartate ||
verses.trans: "But if he meditates on two mātrās (A-U), he attains the mind (manas). The Yajurvedic hymns lead him upward to the intermediate space (antarikṣa) and to the world of the moon (Somaloka). There, having experienced the fullness (vibhūti) of that world, he returns again."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara explains that the second mātrā 'U' (the middle sound, connecting the initial 'A' and the final 'M') corresponds to the dream state, the Yajurveda, and the intermediate space. The dreaming mind is the appropriate domain of 'U' — it is between the gross waking world ('A') and the subtle deep sleep ('M'). Reaching the moon-world through A-U meditation gives the great experience of Soma-loka (heavenly enjoyment), but because the meditator has not reached the third mātrā (M, pointing to nirguna Brahman), they must return. Return is saṃsāra; non-return is mokṣa.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees two-mātrā meditation as reaching the Lord's mental-vital dimension — the Somaloka as the Lord's luminous world accessible through devotional meditation at the mental level. The Yajurveda's liturgical world of ritual action connects to this intermediate level where action-and-devotion work together. The soul that reaches this world experiences 'vibhūti' (divine fullness) — the Lord's abundant grace at this level — but returns because the bhakti has not yet reached the full three-mātrā depth of para-Brahman realisation.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva maps the three mātrās onto three levels of Viṣṇu's cosmic body: the physical world (A), the intermediate vital-mental world (U/Soma), and the highest transcendent world (M/Vaikuṇṭha). The two-mātrā meditator reaches the intermediate Soma-world — a genuinely divine realm of Viṣṇu's śakti — but not Vaikuṇṭha, so return remains necessary. The specificity of 'vibhūti' (divine fullness) in the Soma-world reflects Viṣṇu's grace lavished on souls at each level of approximation to the full truth.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads the second mātrā 'U' as the mental-vital plane — the world of organised mind operating through vital energy. The dream state ('manas') corresponds to this level; the Soma world with its 'vibhūti' (fullness) is the richly luminous mental-vital cosmos that consciousness accesses through inner development. But because this is still a created world, not the supramental source, the soul must eventually descend. The gradient A→U→M maps perfectly onto Aurobindo's schema of material→vital-mental→supramental planes.

verses.dev: यः पुनरेतं त्रिमात्रेणोमित्येतेनैवाक्षरेण परं पुरुषमभिध्यायीत स तेजसि सूर्ये संपन्नः यथा पादोदरस्त्वचा विनिर्मुच्यते एवंह वै स पाप्मना विनिर्मुक्तः स सामभिरुन्नीयते ब्रह्मलोकं स एतस्माज्जीवघनात्परात्परं पुरिशयं पुरुषमीक्षते ॥
verses.iast: yaḥ punaretatrimātreṇomityetenaivākṣareṇa paraṁ puruṣamabhidhyāyīta sa tejasi sūrye saṁpannaḥ yathā pādodarastvacā vinirmucyate evaṁha vai sa pāpmanā vinirmuktaḥ sa sāmabhirunnīyate brahmalokaṁ sa etasmājjīvaghanātparātparaṁ puriśayaṁ puruṣamīkṣate ||
verses.trans: "But whoever meditates on the highest Puruṣa with this same syllable Om in all its three mātrās — he is united with the blazing sun. Just as a snake is freed from its skin, so is he freed from sin. He is led upward by the Sāmavedic hymns to the world of Brahman (Brahmaloka). From that gathered life-mass (jīvaghana) he sees the Puruṣa dwelling in the city (the body) — higher than the high."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara treats this as the climactic teaching: full three-mātrā Om-meditation leads to the realisation of the highest Puruṣa — nirguna Brahman. The snake-skin analogy is perfect: sin (pāpman) is shed like an old skin, revealing the pure Self beneath. Union with the blazing sun means the meditator's consciousness is absorbed into the solar prāṇa-Brahman and goes to Brahmaloka. 'Seeing the Puruṣa higher than the high' is the vision of the nirguṇa Brahman through the saguṇa, which leads to final liberation. This is krama-mukti (gradual liberation) through the highest Upāsanā.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees the three-mātrā meditation as the fullest form of devotion to the Lord — meditating on Om as the sound-body of the Lord in His complete form (both His immanent saguna and His transcendent para aspects). Union with the blazing sun is the Lord's own luminous self-revelation at death. 'Freed from sin like a snake from its skin' describes the complete grace of the Lord removing the devotee's accumulated bondage. Brahmaloka as the Lord's own world gives the liberated soul the beatific vision of the 'Puruṣa higher than the high' — Nārāyaṇa in His transcendent glory.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads three-mātrā meditation as the highest form of Viṣṇu-upāsanā available within the framework of Om. The meditator who reaches this level is completely freed from sin and reaches Brahmaloka — which, in Madhva's highest interpretation, is Vaikuṇṭha itself, the eternal abode of Viṣṇu. 'The Puruṣa higher than the high' seen from this vantage is Viṣṇu in His supreme transcendence — absolutely independent, absolutely perfect, absolutely beyond all else. This vision is the culmination of the soul's entire journey from first enquiry to final liberation.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads the three-mātrā meditation as the complete yoga of the vibrational ground of existence. The third mātrā 'M' corresponds to deep sleep and the causal body — the point where the individual consciousness touches the universal ground. Full three-mātrā meditation thus traverses the entire spectrum from the gross (A) through the subtle (U) to the causal (M), achieving the integral synthesis. 'Freed from sin like a snake from its skin' is the integral transformation: not merely purification but a radical shedding of the old identity to reveal the cosmic Self. 'The Puruṣa higher than the high' — parātpara — is Aurobindo's Brahman that is simultaneously Transcendent, Universal, and Individual.

verses.dev: तस्माद्ओं इत्येतेनैव विदुर्निरोधमनुत्तमं एतज्जागत्यं समिधमेष ब्राह्मणः ॥
verses.iast: tasmādoṁ ityetenaiva viduḥ niroddhamānuttamaṁ etajjāgatyaṁ samidhamesa brāhmaṇaḥ ||
verses.trans: "Therefore those who know this know the supreme cessation (nirodha). Om alone is the inextinguishable fuel of the wise one who lives in the world (Brahman)."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara reads 'supreme cessation' (anuttama nirodha) as mokṣa — the final cessation of the cycle of birth and death. Om is the 'fuel' (samidh) not for a perishable fire but for the inextinguishable fire of Brahman-knowledge. The image of the Brahmin carrying fuel (samidh) to the teacher with which the Upaniṣad began now completes its arc: the true fuel of spiritual life is Om itself, and the inextinguishable fire lit by it is the Self-knowledge that burns away all ignorance. The fuel here feeds the eternal fire rather than burning down to ash.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees 'the supreme cessation' as the attainment of eternal, loving union with the Lord — the final ending of separation, not the end of joyful existence. Om as the inextinguishable fuel of the wise Brahmin is the mantra of unceasing devotion — never exhausted because it draws on the inexhaustible nature of the Lord Himself. The wise one who lives in the world with Om as his constant inner fire never runs dry of devotional energy — the prāṇava fuels bhakti indefinitely.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads 'anuttama nirodha' as the highest liberation — Viṣṇu-sāyujya (joyful proximity to Viṣṇu in Vaikuṇṭha). This supreme cessation ends the cycle of birth and death while initiating the eternal bliss of Viṣṇu-sevā (service to Viṣṇu). Om as 'inextinguishable fuel' for the Brahmin who lives in the world (the Brahmin-universe of Viṣṇu's own reality) is the mantric basis of eternal devotional engagement. The fuel never burns out because it is grounded in Viṣṇu's own inexhaustible nature.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads 'supreme cessation' not as the cessation of existence but as the cessation of ignorance and limitation — the anuttama nirodha of the ego's separative claims. Om as the inextinguishable fuel for the wise one in the world (not outside it) is precisely the Integral vision: the mantric ground of consciousness that sustains the practitioner not in a cave of withdrawal but in the world itself. The Brahmin 'living in the world' with Om as his fuel is the image of the integral yogin — fully engaged in cosmic life while anchored in the transcendent sound-ground of Being.

verses.dev: एतद्वा अमृतं सत्यकाम एतज्जागत्यं एतदपरं च परं च ब्रह्म यदोङ्कारः तस्मादेतेन विद्वानेतेनैवायतनेनैतयोरन्यतरमन्वेति ॥
verses.iast: etadvā amṛtaṁ satyakāma etajjāgatyaṁ etadaparaṁ ca paraṁ ca brahma yadoṅkāraḥ tasmādetena vidvānetenaivāyatanena etayoranyataramanveti ||
verses.trans: "This, Satyakāma, is the immortal — the supportive ground (jāgatya). This Om is indeed both the lower (apara) and the higher (para) Brahman. Therefore the wise one, by this support (āyatana) alone, attains one or the other."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara concludes the fifth praśna by affirming Om's double nature: immortal (amṛta), the supportive ground (jāgatya — the world's ground), encompassing both the saguna and nirguna Brahman. The repetition of the teaching from verse 5.2 creates a deliberate closure — the praśna opens and closes with the same declaration, enclosing the detailed instruction in a frame of Om's comprehensive nature. The 'wise one who attains one or the other' — whether lower or higher Brahman — is the aspirant who uses Om as his vehicle according to the depth of his meditation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja underlines the word 'amṛtam' (immortal): Om is the immortal ground because the Lord whose name it is is immortal. Jāgatya (the world's ground) affirms that Om is not a transcendent-only syllable but the immanent vibrational foundation of the entire manifest cosmos. The world is upheld by the sound-body of the Lord, which is Om. For the devotee, every moment lived in conscious resonance with Om is a moment of alignment with the world's divine ground — the most practical mysticism.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva affirms Om as amṛtam (the immortal sound) — the eternal name-syllable of Viṣṇu who Himself is immortal. 'The ground of the world' (jāgatya) is Viṣṇu's own śakti expressed through His name Om.
