The repetition of the summary teaching (Om = para and apara Brahman, and the wise one attains one or the other) emphasises its centrality. For Madhva, the difference between attaining the lower or the higher Brahman through Om-meditation is the difference between reaching a divine world and reaching Vaikuṇṭha — both real, one surpassing the other.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads 'amṛtam jāgatyam' — the immortal ground of the world — as the supramental Truth-consciousness that is simultaneously beyond time and the foundation of all temporal existence. Om is the vibrational interface between the Transcendent and the Immanent — the 'sound' that the Transcendent makes when it enters Time, and the 'silence' that the Immanent reaches when it returns to Eternity. The Praśna's conclusion on Om is the conclusion of its entire philosophy: the key to both the para and the apara, the immortal and the temporal, the silent and the manifest, is the same syllable — Om.

verses.sec: Praśna 6 — The Puruṣa of Sixteen Parts
verses.dev: अथ हैनं सुकेशा भारद्वाजः पप्रच्छ भगवन् हिरण्यनाभः कौसल्यो राजपुत्रो मामुपेत्य एतं प्रश्नमपृच्छत षोडशकलं भारद्वाज पुरुषं वेत्थेति तं होवाचाहं नैतं वेद यद्यहमिमं अवेदिष्यं कथं ते नावक्ष्यमिति सोऽहमनृतं ब्रुवाणस्समूलो हि परिशुष्यामि तस्मान्न वक्तुमर्हामि मूलं तु मार्गमाणः एहि मा पृक्षेति ॥
verses.iast: atha hainaṁ sukeśā bhāradvājaḥ papraccha bhagavan hiraṇyanābhaḥ kauсalyo rājaputro māmupetya etaṁ praśnamapṛcchata ṣoḍaśakalaṁ bhāradvāja puruṣaṁ vettheti taṁ hovācāhaṁ naitaṁ veda yadyahamimam avediṣyam kathaṁ te nāvakṣyamiti so'hamanṛtaṁ bruvāṇassamūlo hi pariśuṣyāmi tasmānna vaktumarhāmi mūlaṁ tu mārgamāṇaḥ ehi mā pṛkṣeti ||
verses.trans: Then Sukeśā Bhāradvāja asked: "Venerable sir, the prince Hiraṇyanābha of Kośala once came to me and asked: 'Bhāradvāja, do you know the Puruṣa of sixteen parts?' I said to him: 'I do not know him. If I had known him, why would I not have told you? One who speaks untruth dries up from the roots. Therefore I shall not speak untruth.' Then he mounted his chariot and departed in silence. So I ask you — where is that Puruṣa?"

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara admires Sukeśā's intellectual honesty: rather than pretending knowledge he did not have, he admitted ignorance. This is the beginning of true Vedāntic enquiry — the acknowledgment that one does not know. 'One who speaks untruth dries up from the roots' is a remarkable statement about the existential consequences of untruth: falsehood cuts the seeker off from the very ground of Being (which is sat — truth). Sukeśā's question about the 'Puruṣa of sixteen parts' (ṣoḍaśakala) brings in the Sāṃkhya framework, which will now be transformed by Vedāntic understanding.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja finds Sukeśā's integrity exemplary of the bhakti-ideal: rather than embarrass Hiraṇyanābha or lose face by pretending to know, he speaks the truth and admits ignorance. This truthfulness (satya) — praised in every praśna of this Upaniṣad — is an expression of the soul's alignment with the Lord who is Truth itself. Sukeśā's admission that speaking untruth 'dries up from the roots' is a theological statement: untruth separates the soul from its divine source (the Lord who is Sat), causing existential withering.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads Sukeśā's refusal to speak untruth as an act of direct Viṣṇu-dharma. Satya (truth) is the fundamental attribute of right relationship with Viṣṇu — to speak falsely about the Puruṣa of sixteen parts would not be mere error but a spiritual offense (aparādha) against Viṣṇu. 'Drying up from the roots' expresses what Madhva sees as the spiritual consequence of departing from the Lord's nature: the soul that speaks falsehood becomes cut off from the divine current of Being that nourishes it.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo sees Sukeśā's intellectual honesty as the model attitude for the integral seeker: never falsifying one's experience or knowledge to satisfy questioners. 'Truth dries up from the roots' is the most serious spiritual statement about falsehood — untruth severs the connection to the Ground of Being. In Aurobindo's Integral framework, the first requirement of the spiritual life is radical truthfulness (satya) — not as a moral rule but as an ontological necessity: the seeker who falsifies their experience cannot evolve, because evolution requires accurate feedback from the Ground.

verses.sec: Praśna 6 — The Puruṣa of Sixteen Parts
verses.dev: तस्मै स होवाच इहैवान्तःशरीरे सोम्य स पुरुषो यस्मिन्नेताः षोडशकलाः प्रभवन्तीति ॥
verses.iast: tasmai sa hovāca ihaivāntaḥśarīre somya sa puruṣo yasminnetāḥ ṣoḍaśakalāḥ prabhavantīti ||
verses.trans: He said to him: "Dear one, that Puruṣa from whom these sixteen parts spring — he is right here, within this body."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara finds this answer the most quintessentially Upaniṣadic: the cosmic Puruṣa of sixteen parts, which might be imagined as dwelling in some distant heaven, is 'right here, within this body' (ihaiva antaḥśarīre). This is the 'tat tvam asi' movement — the recognition that what appears transcendent and cosmic is in fact the very Self dwelling in the heart. The sixteen parts will emerge from this immanent Puruṣa and will be shown to dissolve back into Him — an image of creation and dissolution at the level of the individual.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads 'right here within the body' as the Lord in His role as antaryāmin (the inner ruler). The Puruṣa of sixteen parts — the supreme Lord who has taken up residence in the body as the inner controller — is not to be sought in distant heavens but in the immediate experience of the body itself. This profound localisation of the divine is central to Viśiṣṭādvaita: the Lord is not merely transcendent but intimately, immanently present in the heart of every being, as their deepest, most interior reality.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads 'within this body' as the location of the individual jīvātman, which is sustained by the Paramātman (Viṣṇu) dwelling within the heart. The Puruṣa of sixteen parts is not the jīva alone but is the cosmos-in-miniature — the individual being as a microcosm of the complete Viṣṇu-governed universe. The sixteen parts that will be enumerated are real components of the individual's being, each an expression of Viṣṇu's śakti. Discovering the Puruṣa means discovering Viṣṇu's presence within — not as identical with the self but as the self's most intimate Lord.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads 'within the body' as pointing to the integral truth that the cosmic and the individual are not separate levels but the same reality at different scales of focussation. The Puruṣa of sixteen parts — the complete cosmic Person — is 'here in the body' because the body is itself a complete cosmic expression, not merely a fragment. The Integral Yoga's physical transformation is grounded exactly in this recognition: the body is not a cage of the spirit but a point of concentration of the cosmic Person, and its transformation is the cosmic Person's self-revelation at the material level.

verses.sec: Praśna 6 — The Puruṣa of Sixteen Parts
verses.dev: स ईक्षांचक्रे कस्मिन्नहमुत्क्रान्त उत्क्रान्तो भविष्यामि कस्मिन्वा प्रतिष्ठिते प्रतिष्ठास्यामीति ॥
verses.iast: sa īkṣāñcakre kasminnаhamutkrānte utkrānto bhaviṣyāmi kasminvā pratiṣṭhite pratiṣṭhāsyāmīti ||
verses.trans: "He (the Puruṣa) reflected: 'When I depart, who will I depart from? When I am established, in what shall I be established?'"

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara finds this verse philosophically profound: the Puruṣa's self-reflection before creating the sixteen parts mirrors Brahman's own eka-vijñāna (single deliberation) before creation in the Chāndogya Upaniṣad. The Puruṣa asks two questions: 'From what do I depart when I leave the body?' and 'In what am I established?' These are questions of ground and departure-point — the very questions that will reveal prāṇa as the gathering hub and the Puruṣa as the hub itself. The Puruṣa's self-reflection is Brahman's own self-contemplation through the individual perspective.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads the Puruṣa's reflection as the Lord's own contemplation of His relationship with the created order. 'When I depart, from what do I depart?' — this is the Lord thinking about the relationship between His transcendent nature and His immanent bodily existence. 'In what shall I be established?' — this is the Lord considering the ground of His own immanence. The answer that will emerge (the sixteen parts flowing from and back into the Puruṣa like spokes from a hub) reveals the Lord as both the source and the destination of all existence.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads the Puruṣa's deliberation as the cosmic Paramātman (Viṣṇu) considering how to arrange the individual being's relationship to its source. 'From what do I depart?' has the answer: from the prāṇa-system that sustains the body. 'In what am I established?' has the answer: in Myself — the Puruṣa is established in the Puruṣa. This self-referential establishment is the Dvaita description of Viṣṇu's absolute self-sufficiency (sva-tantra): He is established in Himself alone, requiring nothing external.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads the Puruṣa's self-reflection as the divine consciousness asking the deepest question about its own creative movement. 'From what do I depart?' — from the material expression. 'In what shall I be established?' — in the supramental consciousness that is its own true nature. The creative act that follows (producing the sixteen parts) is the answer to these questions: the Puruṣa produces all the components of embodied existence in order to have a field for its self-exploration, knowing that all of them will ultimately dissolve back into the Puruṣa itself.

verses.sec: Praśna 6 — The Puruṣa of Sixteen Parts
verses.dev: स प्राणमसृजत प्राणाच्छ्रद्धां खं वायुर्ज्योतिरापः पृथिवीन्द्रियं मनोऽन्नमन्नाद्वीर्यं तपो मन्त्राः कर्म लोका लोकेषु च नाम च ॥
verses.iast: sa prāṇamasṛjata prāṇācchraddhāṁ khaṁ vāyurjyotirāpaḥ pṛthivīndriyaṁ mano'nnamannādvīryaṁ tapo mantrāḥ karma lokā lokeṣu ca nāma ca ||
verses.trans: "He created prāṇa. From prāṇa: faith (śraddhā), space (ākāśa), air (vāyu), light (jyoti), water (āpaḥ), earth (pṛthivī), the sense organs (indriya), mind (manas), food (anna). From food: strength (vīrya), austerity (tapas), mantras, karma, worlds (loka) — and in the worlds, name (nāma)."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara notes that the sixteen parts emerge in a precise cascade: Puruṣa → prāṇa → śraddhā → the five elements → senses, mind, food → vīrya, tapas, mantras, karma, worlds, name. The cascade moves from the subtlest (prāṇa, faith, space) to the grossest (worlds, name). Critically, the list includes both material elements and non-material qualities (faith, austerity, mantras) — showing that the sixteen parts are not just physical components but the complete spectrum of embodied existence: material, vital, mental, moral, and cosmic.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja finds the sixteen parts a complete description of the Lord's body at the individual scale. Śraddhā (faith) as the second part after prāṇa is significant for Viśiṣṭādvaita: faith is the first expression of the vital force in the direction of the divine. The inclusion of karma and mantras as part of the Puruṣa's own emanations shows that ritual action and sacred sound are not external impositions on the self but intrinsic expressions of the divine Person's own creative deployment. The world (loka) with its names completes the cosmic body.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva carefully analyses each of the sixteen parts as a distinct Viṣṇu-śakti operating at a specific level of the created order. The prominence of śraddhā (faith) as the first quality to emerge from prāṇa reflects Madhva's own emphasis on faith in Viṣṇu as the very first requisite for spiritual life. Karma as a distinct part shows that Madhva's theology of karma is not mere moralism but a description of one of Viṣṇu's own emanations — karma belongs to the Puruṣa's own creative order, not to some alien external force.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads the sixteen parts as a complete map of the evolutionary descent of consciousness into matter. The sequence — prāṇa → faith → space → air → fire → water → earth — is the classic Sāṃkhya-Vedāntic account of cosmic evolution; then senses, mind, food, strength, tapas, mantras, karma, worlds, and name complete the picture by adding the psychological, ethical, liturgical, and cosmic dimensions. In the Integral framework, these sixteen are the full spectrum of the divine self-expression that must be transformed, not transcended, in the supramental revolution.

verses.sec: Praśna 6 — The Puruṣa of Sixteen Parts
verses.dev: स यथेमा नद्यः स्यन्दमानाः समुद्रायणाः समुद्रं प्राप्यास्तं गच्छन्ति भिद्येते तासां नामरूपे समुद्रः एवमेवास्य द्रष्टुः इमाः षोडशकलाः पुरुषायणाः पुरुषं प्राप्यास्तं गच्छन्ति भिद्येते चासां नामरूपे पुरुष इत्येवं प्रोच्यते स एष कलारहितोऽमृतो भवतीति ॥
verses.iast: sa yathemā nadyaḥ syandamānāḥ samudrāyaṇāḥ samudraṁ prāpyāstaṁ gacchanti bhidyete tāsāṁ nāmarūpe samudraḥ evamevāsya draṣṭuḥ imāḥ ṣoḍaśakalāḥ puruṣāyaṇāḥ puruṣaṁ prāpyāstaṁ gacchanti bhidyete cāsāṁ nāmarūpe puruṣa ityevaṁ procyate sa eṣa kalārahito'mṛto bhavatīti ||
verses.trans: "Just as these rivers, flowing toward the sea, reach the ocean and disappear — their names and forms dissolve — and only 'sea' is spoken of; in the same way these sixteen parts of the seer (the Puruṣa), flowing toward the Puruṣa, reach the Puruṣa and dissolve — their names and forms dissolve — and only 'Puruṣa' is spoken of. That Puruṣa, free of all parts (kalā-rahita), becomes immortal."

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara considers this the theological culmination of the entire Upaniṣad. The rivers-into-sea image (already used in Praśna 4) here reaches its most pointed application: the sixteen parts are 'rivers' flowing back to the 'ocean' of the Puruṣa. When they dissolve, the Puruṣa remains alone — 'free of all parts' (kalā-rahita). This is the nirguna Brahman — the infinite ground that remains when all its expressions are dissolved. 'Becomes immortal' (amṛto bhavati) is the definitive declaration of liberation: the Puruṣa, recognised as free of all conditioned parts, is immortal Brahman itself.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja carefully reads 'names and forms dissolve' as referring to the dissolution at liberation or pralaya, not as evidence that the parts were unreal. The rivers are real rivers that genuinely merge into a real ocean — they lose their separate names without losing their existence as water. The Puruṣa 'free of parts' at liberation is the Lord in His transcendent aspect — not composed of the world He has manifested but the eternal ground of it. Liberation is the soul's return to the Lord, losing its individual name-and-form in the bliss of divine union while remaining a real, distinct reality in the Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva insists on the river analogy's limits: rivers truly merge into the sea as water, losing only their names not their existence. The sixteen parts dissolve into the Puruṣa not into non-existence but into Viṣṇu's own being — becoming one with His body. The Puruṣa 'free of parts' (kalā-rahita) after this dissolution is Viṣṇu in His absolute transcendence — beyond even His own cosmic expressions. Immortality (amṛtatva) is not the Puruṣa's achievement at liberation but His eternal nature — the sixteen parts' dissolution simply removes the veil that obscured this eternal immortal reality.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads this as the most powerful evolutionary image in the Upaniṣad: the sixteen parts of cosmic existence are not negated but fulfilled by returning to the Puruṣa. The rivers do not cease to be water when they reach the sea — they become the sea. In the Integral framework, this dissolution is the completion of the evolutionary arc: all the cosmic parts (matter, life, mind, karma, world) having fulfilled their evolutionary purpose, dissolve back into the conscious Puruṣa who is now revealed as kalā-rahita — beyond all limitations, immortal — not because the cosmos was false but because it has been completely integrated into the Puruṣa's self-knowledge.

verses.sec: Praśna 6 — The Puruṣa of Sixteen Parts
verses.dev: तद्धैतत् श्लोकेनाभ्युक्तम् अरा इव रथनाभौ कला यस्मिन् प्रतिष्ठिताः तं वेद्यं पुरुषं वेद यथा मा वो मृत्युः परिव्यथा इति ॥
verses.iast: taddhaitat ślokenābhyuktam arā iva rathanābhau kalā yasmin pratiṣṭhitāḥ taṁ vedyaṁ puruṣaṁ veda yathā mā vo mṛtyuḥ parivyathā iti ||
verses.trans: "This is expressed in a verse: 'Know the Puruṣa who is worth knowing, in whom the parts (kalās) are established like spokes in the hub of a wheel — so that death may not afflict you.'"

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara finds the wheel-hub-spokes image (also appearing in the Muṇḍaka and Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣads) the perfect summation of the Puruṣa-teaching. The hub (nābhi) of the wheel is the Puruṣa; the spokes (arā) are the sixteen parts; the rim is the world of experience. The hub does not move even as the wheel turns — the Puruṣa is the unchanging centre around which all phenomenal existence revolves. 'So that death may not afflict you' is the pragmatic statement of the teaching's purpose: knowing the immortal hub, the seeker is freed from the fear of death.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja reads the hub-spoke image as the perfect geometric image of the Lord's cosmic body: the Lord is the hub (the unchanging centre), and all the souls and cosmic parts are His spokes (established in and dependent on Him). The wheel of saṃsāra turns around this hub — but the hub itself is unmoved, the Lord's transcendent nature remaining unaffected by the cosmic revolution. The devotee who 'knows the Puruṣa' in this way is anchored to the hub rather than being carried round and round on the rim — the practical fruit of devotional knowledge is this freedom from the dizzying revolution of saṃsāra.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva finds the hub-spokes image perfectly expressing the Dvaita ontological structure: Viṣṇu is the hub — the centre, the support, the point of convergence — and all jīvas and cosmic parts are the spokes, genuinely distinct yet all radiating from and depending on the central hub. The hub is not the spokes; the spokes are not the hub — but neither exists without the other in the functioning wheel. 'So that death may not afflict you' is the promissory conclusion: the devotee who knows Viṣṇu as this universal hub has found the immortal ground and transcends the death-cycle.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo finds the hub-spokes image the most condensed expression of the Integral cosmology: the Puruṣa as the timeless centre (the hub) with all the dynamic parts of existence (the spokes) radiating from it. The wheel is the perfect image of the Integral life: dynamic (the spokes, the rim, the turning) and yet grounded in stillness (the hub, the axis). 'So that death may not afflict you' is the Integral promise: one who knows the deathless centre can engage fully in the turning of the cosmic wheel without being destroyed by it — this is the integral freedom that combines transcendence and engagement.

verses.sec: Praśna 6 — The Puruṣa of Sixteen Parts
verses.dev: तान् होवाच एतावद्एवाहमेतत्परं ब्रह्म वेद नातः परमस्तीति ॥
verses.iast: tān hovāca etāvadeva' hamtaatparaṁ brahma veda nātaḥ paramastīti ||
verses.trans: "He said to them: 'This is all I know of the supreme Brahman — there is nothing beyond this.'"

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara reads this as Pippalāda's statement of the completeness of the teaching while simultaneously acknowledging that Brahman is beyond all statements. 'This is all I know' is not a confession of limitation but a declaration that the teaching is complete — the Puruṣa of sixteen parts, the hub-spokes image, the dissolution of the parts into the Puruṣa — this is the full content of the highest knowledge available through language. 'There is nothing beyond this' points toward the nirguna Brahman itself, which is indeed beyond all further description.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja takes Pippalāda's statement as the declaration that the teaching of the Puruṣa-of-sixteen-parts is the complete expression of the Lord's nature available to the qualified student at this level. 'There is nothing beyond this' is not an absolute limit but a contextual completion: for these six seekers, at this level of practice and understanding, this is the full teaching that can be given. The Lord's infinity is not exhausted — but Pippalāda has given all that can be given in words, and the rest must come through direct experience in meditation and devotion.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads 'there is nothing beyond this' as Pippalāda's affirmation that Viṣṇu is the highest reality and that the teaching of His nature as the Puruṣa-of-sixteen-parts is the summit of Vedāntic knowledge. This is not a limitation of the teacher but an affirmation of the teaching's completeness: Viṣṇu is the absolute, and Pippalāda has communicated His essential nature as fully as language allows. Beyond this teaching, only direct experience of Viṣṇu in meditation and His grace can take the seeker further.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads Pippalāda's conclusion with deep appreciation: the true teacher, having given the complete teaching, also acknowledges its limits — 'this is all I know' is the intellectual honesty of the fully realised, who knows that no verbal formulation exhausts the Reality. In the Integral framework, this is the characteristic attitude of the supramental realiser: full, complete expression of the truth coupled with the recognition that the living Reality is always more than any statement. The teaching is complete as a teaching; the reality it points to is inexhaustible.

verses.sec: Praśna 6 — The Puruṣa of Sixteen Parts
verses.dev: ते हाभिप्रणम्य पप्रच्छुस्त्वं हि नः पिता योऽस्माकमविद्यायाः परं तारयसि नमस्ते परमऋषिभ्यः ॥
verses.iast: te hābhipraṇamya papracchustvaṁ hi naḥ pitā yo'smākam avidyāyāḥ paraṁ tārayasi namaste paramaṛṣibhyaḥ ||
verses.trans: "They bowed down to him and said: 'You are indeed our father — you who carry us across to the other shore, beyond ignorance. Salutation to the highest seers!'"

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara sees this conclusion as the beautiful resolution of the teaching relationship. The six disciples, having received the complete teaching, recognise Pippalāda not merely as a teacher but as a father (pitā) — the one who, through spiritual birth into knowledge, has given them a more fundamental life than biological parentage. 'Carrying across ignorance' (avidyāyāḥ paraṁ tārayasi) is the traditional image of the guru as the ferryman who takes the disciple from the shore of ignorance (avidyā) to the shore of knowledge (vidyā). The final 'salutation to the highest seers' honors the entire lineage of realised teachers.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Rāmānuja sees the disciples' recognition of Pippalāda as 'father' as the perfect expression of the guru-disciple relationship in the devotional tradition. The guru who carries the disciple 'across ignorance' is performing the Lord's own redemptive function — the Lord uses the guru as His instrument of grace for the seeker's liberation. 'Highest seers' (parama ṛṣis) are those whose vision penetrates to the Lord's own nature. The Upaniṣad ends with praise — not just of Pippalāda but of the entire tradition of realised devotees through whom the Lord's teaching flows.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads this closing verse as a model of the correct attitude toward the guru in the Dvaita tradition. The guru who teaches the truth of Viṣṇu's sovereignty is performing Viṣṇu's own work of liberation — he is thus revered as one's spiritual father, superior to one's biological father. Carrying across the ocean of ignorance (samsāra) to the shore of Viṣṇu-knowledge is the guru's supreme service. The 'highest seers' (parama ṛṣis) are those who have directly realised Viṣṇu — their blessing (namaste) is sought because it is through their grace that the Lord's grace descends.
commentaries.integral_advaita: Aurobindo reads the disciples' recognition of Pippalāda as father as the culmination of a true spiritual transmission. The Upaniṣad began with six seekers approaching with fuel in hand, uncertain — it ends with them transformed, recognising in their teacher the one who has given them the most fundamental birth: birth into self-knowledge. 'Carrying across ignorance' is not escape from the world but the crossing from unconscious immersion in creation to conscious participation in it. The Praśna Upaniṣad concludes with an affirmation of the teaching lineage (parama ṛṣis) through which the truth flows — Aurobindo's own integral realisation stands in this lineage, carrying the teaching forward into the evolutionary future.
